Should you build a "fal for robotics"?

Can you run robots' AI in the cloud and sell it as a service — the honest case. May 2026.

A top-to-bottom read: the answer first, then the reasoning, then every robot type checked one by one, then — if you'd build it anyway — where to start and how big it gets.

The short answer#

fal rents cloud GPUs to run AI models for images and video. The idea here is the same one for robots: run a robot's AI "brain" in the cloud and charge for it. To test whether that's a business, we walked through every kind of robot and the real companies building each one.

What we found: the demand to run robot AI off the device is real and growing — but a defensible independent business that does it in the cloud is rare. Not because it's impossible: a robot can run its own real-time reflexes and still ship the heavier thinking layer to your cloud — that hybrid split is the “fal for robotics” product. The real question is whether anyone would prefer to rent that from an independent company, and mostly they wouldn't. Of the 30 best-fit buyers we screen later, only ~3 currently buy a brain externally (inferred posture, not validated procurement); where offload works, robot makers build the AI themselves, get it free, or guard it as their edge. It helps to separate two value axes. Value-per-real-time-decision (real-time control, weak/jammed links) does get pushed back onto the robot. But value-of-aggregation (fleet-learning, long-horizon planning, semantic understanding) is both high-value and latency-tolerant, so that slice is genuinely cloud-eligible — the narrow part is that the makers mostly capture it themselves. Of 24 robot types, only four support an independent AI business at all, and only one is genuinely cloud-based. The winners own the robots and the AI together.

So, as a pure cloud-serving company: not today. But the picture has real seams — places a focused team can build something. The catch: it’s a build-heavy market (most makers build their own brain), so the motion isn’t selling to people shopping for a brain — it’s intercepting or de-risking a build, a harder sale aimed at the adjacent layer they haven’t staffed. This intercept-a-build motion is the central unproven risk of the whole thesis: we found no cited precedent of a funded robotics team abandoning an in-progress build to rent, switching costs cut against it, and "we own our AI" is a story investors reward — so treat it as the bet to validate, not a settled go-to-market. The rest of this page shows the reasoning, the evidence company by company, and where you'd start if you did it.

First, the one idea everything rests on#

A robot's AI usefully decomposes into three places — when the architecture keeps them separate. They have different needs, and the middle one is the main candidate for the cloud. The catch: the boundary between fast control and the thinking layer depends on architecture, network and safety, and it keeps moving — end-to-end VLAs can blur the two (see A3.5: action chunks execute at 50–200 Hz). So a clean middle to lift to the cloud isn't guaranteed for every robot.

LayerWhere it runsWhat it does / why it runs there
Fast control (reflexes, balance · 50–1000 Hz)On the robot — alwaysCloses the loop in under 50 ms with no buffer. A dropped packet at 200 Hz is a fall or a collision, so it can't ride a best-effort WAN (wired / tethered / deterministic links can host high-rate control, but the public internet can't).
Thinking layer (planning, perception, next move · 1–10 Hz)On the robot or in the cloud ← this whole mapOn some tasks, tolerates an extra ~200–300 ms by deciding several moves at once (action-chunking) — so for those it's the piece that can move off the robot. But a chunk executed blind for 300 ms is exactly what fails in dynamic, contact-rich or reactive work, so cloud-eligibility is per-task, not universal. Where it should run is Tree A (Filter 1); whether you can sell it is Tree B (Filter 2).
Training (learning from fleet data)Off-robot batch compute — nearly alwaysOffline, compute-heavy, no real-time constraint — mostly public cloud, though air-gapped / on-prem / federated setups exist too. A clear off-device workload, not the contested ground.

Keep that middle row in mind. The whole question is two-part: can the thinking layer run in the cloud, and if it does, can you sell it? The next sections are those two filters.

The answer, in full#

The cloud opportunity is real and broad as a place where work happens — across most robots, the thinking layer and the training both pull compute off the device. But an independent, defensible business selling that is narrow. When we checked all 24 robot types against the actual companies, only four have a proven independent AI business today: commercial cleaning, inspection, drones, and defense. The product can ship in several deployment modes — far-cloud, on-prem/near-edge, hybrid (control onboard, thinking/fleet data off-device), or analytics-only. Under the strict pure-cloud definition, only inspection analytics qualifies — cleaning is a hybrid (control onboard, fleet data in the cloud), and the high-value drone and defense autonomy runs on the robot itself — so the strict count is about one. But that hybrid split is the product this page actually defines, and counted that way the set is larger — cleaning, inspection, and parts of drones — so the strict definition narrows the thesis without killing it.

Everywhere else, the AI is built in-house by the robot maker, taken free from open-source, bundled in by a model lab, or wrapped into a full rent-a-robot service. The companies positioned to win today are the ones building the AI model and the robot fleet together — Physical Intelligence, Skild, Figure, Sereact — not a serving middleman. That's not a settled endpoint, though: stacks often disaggregate over time (IBM→Wintel), and if interface standards fragment off NVIDIA/HF (GO gate G3), an independent layer could open.

What would turn this from "wait" into "go"? A few specific things — and today none of them is true. Here's what we're watching: on the right, the reasons it's a hard no right now; on the left, the signals that would change the call.

flowchart TB
  L["Leader signals"]:::neutral
  L --> GO
  L --> KILL
  subgraph GO["GO — going first is justified if these appear (not true yet)"]
    G1["Serving deals with DATA RIGHTS attached  ★ master signal"]:::win
    G2["Learned-policy cert regime + you take liability"]:::win
    G3["Interface standard fragments off NVIDIA / HF"]:::win
    G4["Inference 15-20%+ of robot opex + multi-vendor fleet ~100k"]:::win
    G5["You can raise $1B+ and attach a model / fleet"]:::win
  end
  subgraph KILL["KILL — all true today"]
    K1["Standard slots pre-owned: CUDA/TensorRT, LeRobot"]:::weak
    K2["Buyers default to good-enough free (NVIDIA Isaac, OSS) — but free still has self-host TCO; the real question is whether that TCO is painful enough to outsource"]:::weak
    K3["Data flywheel lives outside your layer"]:::weak
    K4["Right move (be the model+fleet lab) is $1B+ gated"]:::weak
  end
  classDef win fill:#adf0c7,stroke:#087429,color:#08231a
  classDef weak fill:#ffc6c6,stroke:#bd0909,color:#3a0000
  classDef neutral fill:#e7e7e7,stroke:#595959,color:#222
  click K2 href "https://github.com/NVIDIA/Isaac-GR00T" _blank
  click K4 href "https://techcrunch.com/2026/01/14/robotic-software-maker-skild-ai-hits-14b-valuation/" _blank

Two forces sit behind the "no," but be precise about which, because the easy version is wrong. The wrong version is "serving is free, so there's no margin": NVIDIA gives the serving software away to sell silicon, open-source projects make it free, the model labs bundle it. But free software still has to run on GPUs that someone provisions, autoscales, keeps up and optimizes — and that hosting-plus-optimization layer is exactly what fal, Modal and Together charge billions for. Robot inference needs real specialized work on top — real-time action-chunking, streaming multimodal sensors, deterministic latency, a clean edge/cloud split — that a generic LLM-serving project won't hand you. So the supply side is buildable but not automatically defensible — a moat would have to come from data rights, switching costs, or proprietary runtime IP, and the KILL gates below say those aren't in hand yet. The real "no" is on the demand side: the highest-value inference runs onboard (real-time loops, contested links), so the cloud-serviceable slice is narrow to begin with, and within it the robot makers overwhelmingly build or bundle the brain themselves (of the 30 best-fit buyers we screened, 20 build in-house). NVIDIA, open source and the labs don't squeeze your margin so much as hand every would-be customer a good-enough free default, so almost no one is shopping to rent. The second force is real and unchanged: the one move that captures the whole stack — owning the AI model and the robots — is what PI (~$11B), Skild (~$14B) and Figure (~$39B) are valued at, which is what investors price the move at, not what it costs to make. The capital to enter a narrow wedge is far smaller — a 5–10-person team can build an autonomy stack — so the gate is strategic (owning model and fleet), not a literal $1B build. The bar, then, isn't "build good serving software" (you can); it's that the rentable demand is thin (onboard placement + makers build), and the move that captures the full stack is a $1B+ bet.

Filter 1 — where can a robot's brain actually live?#

Walk any robot through these questions and you learn whether its thinking can run in the cloud. Most of the time the answer is "on the robot": real-time control plus a weak or jammed network forces the AI back on-device. Diamonds are decisions; the coloured boxes are the outcomes (green runs off-robot, red stays onboard, yellow is a mix).

flowchart TB
  classDef q fill:#c6dcff,stroke:#305bab,color:#0a2a5e
  classDef det fill:#e9defb,stroke:#6b46c1,color:#2e1065
  classDef cloud fill:#adf0c7,stroke:#0a5a22,color:#08231a
  classDef hybrid fill:#fff6b6,stroke:#af7e02,color:#3a2e00
  classDef onb fill:#ffc6c6,stroke:#8a0606,color:#3a0000
  classDef term fill:#f1d6ff,stroke:#7d3c98,color:#2e1065

  A1{"A1 · Lifecycle stage? (maturity — see the reference section at the end)"}:::q
  A2{"A2 · Hard regulatory / data bar?"}:::q
  A25{"A2.5 · Is that inadequate connectivity improvable here?"}:::q
  A3{"A3 · Connectivity p99 reliable? (illustrative defaults — task/sensor-dependent; high-res/LiDAR payloads need more) RTT ≤50 ms · jitter ≤20 ms · uplink ≥10 Mbps · loss under 0.1%"}:::q
  A35["A3.5 · Decoder family — determinant, not a fork: sets A4 chunk size + A5 quantizability (families in the glossary)"]:::det
  A4{"A4 · Dropped chunk bounded by an onboard safe-fallback?"}:::q
  A4H{"A4.H · Certified onboard abort policy for link loss?"}:::q
  A5{"A5 · Compute fit: FP16 ≤ onboard VRAM AND ≥ required inference rate within 40–130 W? (memory-fit is the gate, but placement also weighs TCO / power / thermal / utilization / update-velocity, not VRAM alone)"}:::q
  A6["A6 · Offload tier — menu, not a fork: on-prem-edge / near-edge-MEC / far-cloud / hybrid (all → security gate)"]:::det
  ASEC{"A6.SEC · Homogeneous fleet: signed commands + per-robot authz + onboard kill-switch?"}:::q
  AS{"A6.S · Permanent or transitional?"}:::q
  PON["ONBOARD"]:::onb
  PQO["QUANTIZED-ONBOARD"]:::hybrid
  HCOL["HYBRID-COLLAPSED → onboard-or-nothing"]:::term
  SECB["SECURITY-BLOCKED → force onboard"]:::term
  MIT["CLOUD/EDGE + blast-radius mitigation (rate-limit · canary · anomaly-stop)"]:::hybrid
  PROV["Provision private-5G / wired (capex, T5) → connectivity now reliable"]:::hybrid
  PERM["CLOUD / EDGE placement — PERMANENT"]:::cloud
  TRANS["CLOUD / EDGE placement — TRANSITIONAL → Filter 2 capture"]:::cloud
  TELE["TRANSITIONAL-TELEOP — human-in-loop bridge"]:::hybrid

  A1 -->|"Bootstrapping (under 10 units)"| TELE
  A1 -->|"Piloting or scaled (scaling biases onboard / build-in-house — see the reference section at the end)"| A2
  A2 -->|"Must process locally (FDA in-loop, some biometric)"| PON
  A2 -->|"None / soft, or satisfiable by remedy (remedies in the glossary)"| A3

  A3 -->|"Intermittent (p99 fails / coverage holes)"| A4H
  A3 -->|"None / inadequate (≤4G, RTT 30–70 ms)"| A25
  A25 -->|"Yes — fixed site, can install private-5G / CBRS"| PROV
  A25 -->|"No — mobile / outdoor"| A5
  PROV --> A35
  A35 --> A4
  A4 -->|"No — fails dangerous on drop"| PON
  A4 -->|"Yes — safe pause / human absorbs"| A4H
  A4H -->|"Yes — certified abort (Cat-3 PL-d), or force-limited only"| A5
  A4H -->|"None"| HCOL
  A5 -->|"Full FP16 fits onboard (and TCO/power/thermal favor onboard)"| PON
  A5 -->|"Fits only after INT4/INT8"| PQO
  A5 -->|"Too big → offload"| A6
  A6 --> ASEC
  ASEC -->|"All three present"| AS
  ASEC -->|"Some but not all"| MIT
  ASEC -->|"None / shared creds"| SECB
  MIT --> AS
  AS -->|"Permanent (scaled, or fixed by law/physics)"| PERM
  AS -->|"Transitional (re-eval triggers T1–T12 — see the reference section at the end)"| TRANS

  click A3 href "https://www.5gamericas.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/07/5G_Americas_URLLLC_White_Paper_Final__updateJW.pdf" _blank
  click A35 href "https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.07339" _blank
  click A5 href "https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/autonomous-machines/embedded-systems/jetson-thor/" _blank
  click TELE href "https://about.doordash.com/en-us/news/doordash-and-coco-expand-global-partnership" _blank
  click TRANS href "https://modal.com/blog/physical-intelligence-runs-real-time-remote-inference-for-robotic-control-on-modal" _blank

One nuance the tree builds in: a robot program's stage only sets a starting lean. Early on — a handful of units, a human still in the loop — work leans off-robot. As a fleet scales and proves out, it earns the right to move the AI onboard — which cuts both ways for a cloud vendor: scale can push the AI onboard (your best customers churn out of the cloud layer) or it can favor centralized fleet infrastructure (they stay). The direction is unproven, so treat it as a key risk rather than a settled lean. The full thresholds are in the reference section at the bottom.

Filter 2 — even off the robot, can you sell it?#

Running in the cloud isn't a business by itself. Even when a robot's thinking runs off-device, the robot maker usually builds that AI themselves or gets it free. This filter asks whether they'd buy it from an independent company — and whether that company could defend the position.

flowchart TB
  classDef q fill:#c6dcff,stroke:#305bab,color:#0a2a5e
  classDef win fill:#adf0c7,stroke:#0a5a22,color:#08231a
  classDef mid fill:#fff6b6,stroke:#af7e02,color:#3a2e00
  classDef no fill:#ffc6c6,stroke:#8a0606,color:#3a0000
  classDef det fill:#e9defb,stroke:#6b46c1,color:#2e1065

  E["Entry: the thinking layer runs off the robot (from Filter 1)"]:::q
  E --> B0{"B0 · Base-model license allows 3rd-party serving?"}:::q
  B0 -->|"Internal-only / no redistribution"| L1b["Owner / lab captures — no independent"]:::no
  B0 -->|"Open / redistributable / own base"| B1{"B1 · Serving bundled with the licensed model?"}:::q
  B1 -->|"Yes (lab ships the runtime)"| L1["BUNDLED — model lab captures"]:::no
  B1 -->|"No (BYO runtime)"| BX{"BX · Is serving a CORE differentiator, or commodity context?"}:::q
  BX -->|"Core — your moat (passes ⓐ below)"| LBUILD["BUILD in-house — fund/hire the team; owner captures"]:::no
  BX -->|"Context — commodity (fails ⓐ below)"| B2["B2 · Does free OSS / NVIDIA hit the required inference rate? — determinant, not a leaf: yes → 'build' = adopt + self-operate OSS (no license fee, but real infra + ops + people); no → 'build' = write the serving stack from scratch"]:::det
  B2 --> BE{"BE · All-in BUILD (self-operate) vs all-in BUY (managed / license), at scale × duration? (conditions ⓑ below)"}:::q
  BE -->|"BUILD cheaper — all ⓑ-build conditions hold"| L6["BUILD / self-operate — adopt free OSS if it hits the rate, else build from scratch; hire with the buy budget (real TCO)"]:::no
  BE -->|"BUY cheaper — any ⓑ-buy condition holds"| B5{"B5 · Must self-host, or is managed SaaS OK?"}:::q
  B5 -->|"Must self-host (residency / IP)"| L5["BUY a serving LICENSE (on-prem)"]:::win
  B5 -->|"Managed SaaS OK"| B6{"B6 · Vendor depth?"}:::q
  B6 -->|"Generic GPU cloud"| L4a["BUY generic GPU cloud — commodity (Modal/Baseten)"]:::mid
  B6 -->|"VLA-specific serving / runtime"| L4b["BUY VLA-specific serving ★ the 'fal-for-robotics' business"]:::win
  B6 -->|"Full RaaS"| L4c["BUY full RaaS — integrator captures"]:::no
  L4b --> MOAT{"B7 · Moat? switching cost / data-rights / safety-cert"}:::q
  L5 --> MOAT
  MOAT -->|"Yes"| DEF["DEFENSIBLE independent business"]:::win
  MOAT -->|"No"| NDB["NO DEFENSIBLE BUSINESS — commodity, gets absorbed"]:::no

  click L4b href "https://tryreflex.ai/" _blank
  click L1 href "https://github.com/Physical-Intelligence/openpi" _blank

Two questions decide it. First: is the AI your moat, or just plumbing? "Core" doesn't automatically mean "build" — it sets the default toward owning it, but the real call is graded (own / co-develop / license / managed-service), weighing strategic importance against internal capability, any vendor speed/cost/certification advantage, and switching- and re-cert risk. If it's a genuine competitive edge — something customers feel and rivals can't copy — the lean is to build or co-develop in-house, and the room for an independent shrinks (firms still outsource even strategic pieces when a vendor is decisively better, faster, or already certified — which is exactly the intercept-a-build thesis below). It tilts toward sourcing only if every test below fails.

Serving is CORE → build in-house (any one)Serving is CONTEXT → source it (all of these)
Measurable competitive weapon — your latency / throughput / $-per-inference beats rivals and customers feel it (higher pick rate, lower $/pick, more uptime)Customers neither see nor pay for it — undifferentiated plumbing
Embeds proprietary IP — custom kernels, distillation, action-head runtime, model-specific scheduler that competitors can't copyAn off-the-shelf runtime (vLLM / TensorRT / NIM / openpi) already hits your required inference rate
Safety / certification-critical and you must own the evidence chain + carry the liabilityNo proprietary IP is lost by outsourcing it
Co-designed with your model — runtime and weights can't be cleanly separatedCleanly separable from your model (stable API boundary)
Owning it changes your unit economics at your scale (already at graduation scale — see the reference section at the end)Below graduation scale — owning it changes nothing yet

Second, even if it is plumbing: cheaper to build or to buy? Free open-source lowers the acquisition cost (no license fee) — but not the real TCO: infra, autoscaling, on-call ops, validation, observability, safety and support all remain. The buy case wins below real scale, when speed matters, or when requirements are still moving. The intuition that buyers "graduate to building it themselves" at scale is an assumption, not a law — it needs cohort evidence, and some scaled buyers instead standardize on a vendor precisely to stay focused on their core.

BUY cheaper (any one)BUILD / self-operate cheaper (all of these)
Utilization below the crossover — under ~50–60% sustained GPU util / under ~6 GPU-hr/day per accelerator (idle reserved capacity is wasted; managed serverless scales to zero)Sustained high utilization ≥ ~50–60% / over ~6 GPU-hr/day → owned or reserved hardware beats on-demand (break-even ~13–18 mo)
Sub-scale fleet — too few robots / inferences to amortize a dedicated cluster + an ML-infra team (~$0.5–1.5M/yr loaded for 2–4 engineers)Large, stable, homogeneous fleet at graduation scale (100s–1,000s units — see the reference section at the end) to amortize cluster + team
Speed — you need it in weeks, not the 6–12 mo to build, harden, and put a serving stack on-callStable requirements — quarterly (not weekly) retrains, fixed model family (T7 / T12)
Volatile requirements — model family / size still churning; premature to optimize a fixed in-house stackResidency / IP / latency already force self-host (overlaps B5) — marginal cost of also operating is low
Not core (decided by the moat-vs-plumbing test above) and engineering time has higher-value uses; preserve runway (opex over capex)All-in build TCO (hire + build + maintain + infra) < all-in buy (license / managed × fleet × duration)

We checked every robot type — all 24#

Theory is cheap, so we went through all 24 kinds of robots and the real companies in each, and asked of every one: does an independent AI-brain business actually exist here, or do the robot makers build it themselves? (FC = fast control · TL = thinking layer · TR = training.) The verdict column is the follower's read — does an independent vendor exist today with real customers — so a NO comes in two flavours: structural (physics or regulation rules it out for anyone: surgery, telepresence, exoskeletons, space, on-device consumer home) and "not yet" (makers build it in-house today and no independent has traction — e.g. hospital logistics, sidewalk delivery, elder-care; a leader watches the GO/NO-GO gates below for these).

SegmentUse cases (full enumeration)Companies (top 3 → full list in the directory)Per-layerIndep. business?
Stationary / manipulation
1. Fixed industrial armsArc/spot/laser/seam welding; painting, powder/e-coat, sealant; adhesive/bead dispensing, gasket forming; material handling, part transfer, load/unload; palletizing & depalletizing (cases/sacks/drums/mixed); machine tending (CNC, press, injection-molding, die-cast, forging, grinding); assembly (insertion, snap-fit, press-fit, riveting); screwdriving/nut-running; deburring/grinding/sanding/polishing; cutting (laser/waterjet/plasma), trimming, drilling; hemming (auto-body); packaging/case-pack/cartoning; thermal/plasma spray, shot peening; fluid metering; in-line metrology; battery cell/module stacking & welding; foundry/heavy handling (800+ kg).FANUC, Yaskawa (Motoman), Kawasaki Robotics (Kawasaki Heavy Industries) — +108 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard · TR offlinePARTIAL — OEMs build & own vision in-house; thin independent vision layer (Mech-Mind; Photoneo is a 3D sensor supplier, not a software-only thinking-layer vendor per the document's own non-ICP rule)
2. AI pick / pack armsEach/piece picking & put (e-comm); goods-to-person/robot; bin picking (structured + random/cluttered), tote picking; order consolidation, batch/multi-order; kitting & sub-assembly; item validation, barcode-on-pick, weigh-on-pick; pack-out / put-wall; apparel/polybag/blister/transparent-item handling; serialized pharma picking; returns processing, decanting.RightHand Robotics, Covariant, Dexterity — +66 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL on-prem/hybrid · TR cloudPARTIAL — leaders build own VLA; real but thin licensor tail (Fizyr, Apera, Oxipital)
3. Stationary latency-tolerantDepal/singulation/sortation: mixed-case depal, parcel singulation, induction, truck load/unload, case picking. Laundry/chores: folding, sorting, dishwasher load, table-clearing. Food/kitchen: grilling/frying, wok cooking, bowl/salad/burrito/pizza assembly, Autocado, portioning, plating, makeline, ghost-kitchen. Barista: espresso, specialty drinks, boba, café kiosk. Dishwashing/bussing. Lab automation/cloud-labs: pipetting, aliquoting, NGS prep, PCR setup, HTS, plate handling, cell culture, colony picking, remote experiment execution. Pharmacy: Rx fill/count, blister pack, vial fill, ADCs, IV/chemo compounding, unit-dose.Plus One Robotics, Pickle Robot, Ambi Robotics — +311 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL hybrid/far-cloud viable · TR cloudPARTIAL — food/cooking core builds in-house; lab + bin-picking have independents (Mech-Mind, Mujin, Artificial)
4. CobotsMachine tending, palletizing, pick-and-place, assembly, precision screwdriving/fastening, insertion, dispensing/gluing/sealing, cobot arc-welding, finishing (sand/polish/deburr), vision QA/sorting, packaging, lab/pharma handling, soldering, kitting.Universal Robots, Kassow Robots, Techman Robot — +57 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL on-prem/onboard · TR offline + app-storePARTIAL — OEM autonomy in-house; retrofit/skill layer sold (Micropsi, Apera, Generalist)
5. Surgical / medicalSoft-tissue (lap/single-port: prostatectomy, hysterectomy, hernia, cholecystectomy, suturing/anastomosis); orthopedic (knee/hip/shoulder arthroplasty, haptic milling); spine/neuro (pedicle screws, fusion, stereotactic, DBS, laser ablation); endoluminal (bronchoscopy, colonoscopy); aquablation (BPH); ophthalmic (cataract, retinal); super-microsurgery/reconstruction; dental implant drilling; hair FUE; stereotactic radiosurgery.Intuitive Surgical (da Vinci, Ion), Medtronic (Hugo soft-tissue robot), Mazor Robotics (spine; part of Medtronic) — +116 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard only · TR locked/offlineNO — FDA-locked; every OEM owns the full stack
Mobile — ground
6. Warehouse AMRsShelf/tote-to-picker (G2P), dense eaches, returns, replenishment buffering, zone-skipping, peak surge; point-to-point tote, pallet shuttle, cross-dock, cube/grid storage, line-side delivery; pallet putaway/retrieval, VNA, trailer load/unload, high-bay; long-distance multi-cart hauling, milk-run kitting (tugger); parcel sortation, tilt-tray/cross-belt, returns sort.Amazon Robotics, Symbotic, Locus Robotics — +216 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL on-prem/hybrid · TR cloudPARTIAL — leaders build SLAM/fleet-OS in-house; AGV-retrofit licensing exists (BlueBotics ANT), though BlueBotics is now ABB-owned and not fully independent
7. Commercial cleaningAutonomous floor scrubbing (retail/airports/malls/hospitals/schools); industrial sweeping in logistics/factories; large-format floor care (up to 3,900 m²/hr); overnight unattended; carpet vacuuming; route auto-gen; retrofit autonomy on manual scrubbers; cleaning + shelf-scan capture.Avidbots, Tennant Company, Nilfisk — +57 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard/on-prem · TR cloudYES — Brain Corp/BrainOS licensed across Tennant, Nilfisk, SoftBank, ICE — a single proven hybrid autonomy-OS case (one vendor, control onboard / fleet data in the cloud), not proof of broad cloud-cognition demand; Brain Corp is also drifting toward retail data (ShelfOptix) and Gausium is vertically integrating
8. Hospital / indoor logisticsMedication delivery, lab-sample transport, surgical-supply/instrument delivery, meal/tray delivery, soiled-linen & biohazard removal, central-supply restock, mobile manipulation (doors/elevators), OR logistics.ST Engineering Aethon (TUG), Relay Robotics (formerly Savioke), Diligent Robotics (Moxi) — +58 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL on-prem/hybrid · TR cloudNO — full robot-as-a-service; most makers build their own brain (Aethon, Relay, MiR, Vecna); Moxi was rebuilt on NVIDIA GR00T foundation models rather than a fully in-house stack, showing some external dependency, but no independent thinking-layer vendor has traction here
9. Retail shelf-scanOut-of-stock detection, planogram compliance, shelf-tag/price verification (barcode+OCR), SKU localization/mapping, promo-display audit, low-stock alerts, full-store scans (under 60 min/45k sq ft), fixed-sensor zone monitoring, merchandising intelligence.Simbe Robotics (Tally), Badger Technologies (Marty, Jabil division), Brain Corp (BrainOS Sense Suite / ShelfOptix) — +30 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL far-cloud/hybrid · TR cloudPARTIAL — makers build CV/analytics in-house; only nav-autonomy licensed (Brain Corp)
10. Security patrolIndoor facility patrol (offices/data-centers/warehouses), outdoor perimeter/critical-infra, parking/mall surveillance, license-plate recognition, anomaly/intrusion, thermal & gas detection, tailgating detection, telepresence escalation, yard monitoring, dock-launched drone overwatch.Knightscope, Cobalt AI (Cobalt Robotics), Robotic Assistance Devices (RAD / AITX) — +69 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL hybrid/far-cloud (fleet-security gate applies) · TR cloudPARTIAL — leaders build RaaS; thin defense-autonomy tail (Palladyne, Onyx, Levatas)
11. Sidewalk / last-mile deliveryFood/restaurant, grocery & convenience, campus, dense-urban, pharmacy/parcel last-100m, platform fulfillment (Uber Eats/DoorDash).Starship Technologies, Serve Robotics, Coco Robotics — +46 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL hybrid+teleop · TR cloudNO — most operators build their own autonomy (vertically integrated RaaS); Magna and Mitsubishi use Cartken's autonomy stack rather than building in-house, but Cartken is itself a vertically integrated robot maker (not a pure-play thinking-layer vendor), so no independent brain licensor has traction here
12. Road delivery / robotaxis / AVsDriverless ride-hailing, airport runs, purpose-built shuttles, Uber/Lyft dispatch; long-haul hub-to-hub trucking (Sun Belt corridors), refrigerated/food freight, middle-mile B2B; driverless grocery/parcel road delivery.Waymo, Zoox (Amazon), Cruise (GM) — +94 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard only (driving) · TR cloud/simPARTIAL — top operators build in-house; real licensors exist (Mobileye, Nuro, Plus)
13. Agricultural / fieldLaser/mechanical weeding, herbicide-free row weeding, weeding-as-a-service; strawberry/grape/apple/greenhouse harvesting, multi-arm picking; see-and-spray (up to 95% chem reduction), spray drones, refill-station 24/7; crop scouting, weed/pest/disease detection, stand counts, yield mapping; driverless tillage/planting/harvest, RTK auto-steer, orchard/vineyard autonomy.Carbon Robotics, FarmWise, Verdant Robotics — +166 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard/quantized (no rural conn) · TR cloudPARTIAL — leaders build/acquire (Deere); autonomy-software tail (Agtonomy, Bonsai)
14. ConstructionCAD-to-concrete floor layout, stakeout, RTK survey; CAD-guided bricklaying (200–1,000/hr), mortar application; selective/interior demolition, concrete breaking, hazardous-space removal; Autowalk progress scanning, 360° reality capture, confined-space inspection, deviation/rework; rebar tying (~8× faster), autonomous excavation/grading/dozing, drywall taping-mudding-sanding.Built Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, Gravis Robotics — +127 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard/hybrid · TR cloudPARTIAL — incumbents build in-house; retrofit-autonomy tail (Field AI, Teleo, SafeAI)
15. Mining / subsea / hazardousAutonomous ultra-class haul trucks (290t), drilling/dozing/excavation, underground LHD, haulage fleet mgmt, mine inspection; resident seabed drones (12+ mo), pipeline/structure survey, ROV intervention, AUV mapping, topside inspection, naval mine-countermeasures; Zone-1 explosive-atmosphere inspection, gauge/leak/thermal, EOD, nuclear inspection, confined-space patrol.Sandvik (Mining & Rock Solutions / AutoMine), Epiroc, Caterpillar (Cat MineStar) — +219 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard/quantized (no/subsea conn) · TR cloud-on-surfacePARTIAL — Cat/Komatsu build AHS in-house; retrofit tail (Pronto.ai, ASI)
Legged / air / consumer / defense
16. HumanoidsManufacturing: body/parts handling, line material delivery, sequencing/kitting, machine tending, QA, fastening, bin sorting, lights-out shifts, battery-swap. Logistics: tote moving, trailer/truck unload, case induction, palletizing, WMS handling. Retail: shelf stocking, audits, restocking, greeting/wayfinding. Home: laundry, tidying, fetching, doors/lights, dishwashing. Hospitality: housekeeping, luggage, concierge, room-service.Tesla (Optimus), Figure AI, Boston Dynamics — +144 more in the directory →FC onboard (Helix S1 200 Hz) · TL HYBRID — canonical · TR cloudPARTIAL — leaders build brain in-house; PI/Skild/GR00T sell to the long tail
17. Quadruped inspectionOil & gas / ATEX Zone-1 patrols, gas-leak detection, fired-heater thermal scans, valve/gauge reading, power-plant/substation inspection, offshore rig monitoring, nuclear decommissioning, mining surveys, construction progress, night rounds, perimeter security, data-center inspection, spill/debris detection (~25k deployed).Boston Dynamics (Spot), Ghost Robotics (Vision 60), ANYbotics (ANYmal) — +79 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard/hybrid · TR cloudYES — legs in-house, but independent inspection AI sells across fleets (Energy Robotics, Levatas)
18. Drones / UAVsDelivery: medical/blood/Rx, food, parcel, backyard, locker, mall hubs. Inspection: power-line/substation/pipeline, bridge/dam/building, confined-space (boiler/tank/sewer), wind/solar, drone-in-a-box. Ag-spray: crop spraying, sowing, swarm spraying, docking recharge. Mapping: survey-grade photogrammetry, LiDAR, GCP-free, volumetric/stockpile, corridor, digital twins. Security: drone-as-first-responder, perimeter, thermal, event, police overwatch. Defense-air: loitering munitions, anti-armor, ISR, loyal-wingman CCA, counter-UAS, EW.Zipline, Wing (Alphabet), Flytrex — +352 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard/hybrid (analytics) · TR cloudYES — flagships build, but real autonomy/analytics licensors (Auterion, Shield AI, DroneDeploy)
19. Consumer homeVacuuming/mopping, obstacle-avoid, auto-empty; lawn mowing (LiDAR/RTK/AI-vision, boundary-free); pool floor/wall/waterline cleaning; window cleaning; home companionship/pet interaction.iRobot (Roomba), Roborock, Ecovacs Robotics — +202 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard (cost/privacy) · TR cloudNO — most OEMs build on-device intelligence; brain not separable for leading brands; white-label and smaller brands license nav from 3irobotix or Slamtec SLAMWARE, but these are nav component licensors, not independent cloud-cognition vendors, and none has an independent thinking-layer business
20. Social / service / elder-careCompanion conversation, med/appointment reminders, wellness tracking, cognitive engagement, fall/home monitoring, comfort; food running/serving, bussing, room-service, host/greeting, ad displays, drink prep; hospital supply/sample/med transport, EVS, disinfection, nurse-assist logistics; ground security rounds.Bear Robotics, Pudu Robotics, Keenon Robotics — +96 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard/hybrid (companion LLM cloud) · TR cloudNO — makers build the nav brain; only generic cloud LLMs bundled in. Follower's read: zero in-segment defection today. The ICP section treats the hand-built dialogue/orchestration layer as structurally inevitable to rent (Tier 1 beachhead), but that is a leader's forward-looking call — no independent vendor has traction here yet.
21. TelepresenceRemote meetings/walkthroughs, autonomous office nav, remote teaching, hospital tele-rounding/telehealth, remote facility inspection, travel-emission reduction.Double Robotics, OhmniLabs (Ohmni / OhmniCare), Ava Robotics — +38 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL hybrid+teleop (human IS the layer) · TR minimalNO — the remote human is the layer
22. Exoskeletons / wearablesIndustrial lift/lower assist, back-injury prevention, overhead-work support, logistics load handling, AI-adaptive lift; gait training, SCI/stroke/neuro rehab, standing/walking assist; hiking/running/endurance assist, load-carrying, urban mobility, search-and-rescue.Ekso Bionics, ReWalk Robotics (Lifeward), Wandercraft — +122 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard (body-worn, medical regulatory bar) · TR cloudNO — on-body safety-critical control; medical-locked, in-house
23. Defense / militaryGround UGV: logistics/resupply, CASEVAC, ISR, RWS weapons, route-clearance/EOD, robotic combat vehicles, off-road maneuver, armed quadruped. Maritime USV/UUV: anti-submarine, ISR, mine-countermeasures, autonomous strike (VLS), undersea mothership, persistent surveillance.Milrem Robotics, Rheinmetall (American Rheinmetall / Mission Master), QinetiQ — +144 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard (contested comms/EW) · TR cloud/simEMERGING — gov't open architecture + prototype OTAs (Overland AI, Forterra, Kodiak); Army RCV is not yet a program-of-record (award cancelled May 2025; Navy MASC also cancelled), and revenue is OTA/services-heavy early, not yet a proven recurring product business
24. Space / planetaryPlanetary rovers, lunar terrain vehicles, robotic drilling/sampling, in-space servicing/assembly/manufacturing (ISAM), on-orbit refuel/repair/tug/deorbit, satellite servicing & inspection, tower/antenna/solar assembly, ISS arm ops, astronaut-assist / teleoperated avatars, module relocation.Northrop Grumman SpaceLogistics, Motiv Space Systems, Maxar Space Systems (Vantor / Maxar Intelligence robotics) — +85 more in the directory →FC onboard · TL onboard + ground-teleop (light-delay) · TR ground/simNO — bespoke, prime-owned, custom autonomy
The tally across all 24 — YES — a proven independent thinking-layer business exists today (4): commercial cleaning (Brain Corp/BrainOS across Tennant, Nilfisk, SoftBank, ICE), quadruped inspection (Energy Robotics, Levatas across mixed fleets), drones (Auterion, Shield AI Hivemind, DroneDeploy), defense ground/maritime (Overland AI, Forterra, Kodiak — the Army open architecture decouples autonomy from the chassis). Note: the four are distinct product types. Inspection is cloud-cognition/analytics — the only genuinely cloud-native case. Cleaning is a hybrid autonomy-OS (control onboard, fleet data in the cloud). High-value drone and defense autonomy runs onboard as licensed software. Pure cloud-cognition proven count: ~1. PARTIAL — leaders build the brain in-house; a thinner independent tail buys (12): fixed-arm vision (Mech-Mind, Photoneo), AI pick/pack (Fizyr, Apera, Oxipital), food/lab/pharmacy, cobots (Micropsi, Generalist), warehouse AMRs (BlueBotics), retail shelf-scan (nav-autonomy only), security patrol (Palladyne, Onyx, Levatas), road AVs (Mobileye, Nuro, Plus), agricultural (Agtonomy, Bonsai), construction (Field AI, Teleo), mining (Pronto.ai, ASI Mining), humanoids (PI, Skild, GR00T → the tail). NO — vertically integrated, regulated, or the human/onboard is the layer (8): surgical (FDA-locked), hospital logistics (full RaaS), sidewalk delivery (vertically integrated), consumer home (on-device, OEM), social/elder-care (only cloud LLMs bundled in; the ICP screen flags the hand-built dialogue/orchestration layer as the rentable wedge), telepresence (human is the layer), exoskeletons (on-body, medical), space (bespoke, prime-owned).

And the evidence behind those verdicts, company by company — who builds the brain in-house, who buys it from an independent, and whether any independent vendor has real traction. This is the follower's view — who's buying today; for a first-mover, "none yet" is expected, and what a leader bets on instead is the dated recommendation in the founder section below.

SegmentVerdictBuilds the brain in-houseBuys from an independentIndependent vendor(s) with traction
1. Fixed industrial armsPARTIALFANUC, ABB, Yaskawa, KUKA, ComauFANUC/ABB/Yaskawa/KUKA → Mech-Mind; Multi-brand integrators → PhotoneoMech-Mind Robotics, Photoneo
2. AI pick / packPARTIALCovariant, Dexterity, RightHand Robotics, Nimble Robotics, Ambi RoboticsRobot OEMs/integrators → Apera AI; System integrators / OEMs in logistics → FizyrFizyr, Apera AI, Oxipital AI
3. Stationary / food / labPARTIALChef Robotics, Miso Robotics, Posha / Hyphen / Cafe X, Emerald Cloud Lab, Ginkgo BioworksBeckman Coulter → Artificial Inc — partners to layer Artificial's orchestration software on Beckman hardware; Various integrators → Mech-Mind / Mujin / XYZ Robotics — license robot-agnostic 3D-vision + motion-planning 'brain' for bin-pickingMech-Mind Robotics, Mujin, Artificial Inc / Synthace
4. CobotsPARTIALUniversal Robots, Techman Robot, NEURA Robotics, Flexiv, FANUCUniversal Robots → Generalist AI + Scale AI — a leader explicitly sourcing the brain/training layer from independents; Universal Robots / FANUC / KUKA / Doosan / Yaskawa / Kassow → Micropsi IndustriesApera AI, Micropsi Industries, Generalist AI
5. SurgicalNOIntuitive Surgical, Medtronic, Stryker, Zimmer Biomet, Smith+Nephew—none
6. Warehouse AMRsPARTIALAmazon Robotics, Symbotic, Locus Robotics, Geek+, Hai RoboticsTraditional AGV/forklift/tugger OEMs → BlueBotics ANT — 'ANT driven' program; Smaller/mixed fleets → Meili Robots / Open-RMF — vendor-agnostic orchestration exists but with limited real-world adoption; not used by the leadersBlueBotics ANT
7. Commercial cleaningYESAvidbots, Gausium / Gaussian Robotics, Pudu Robotics, Adlatus RoboticsTennant → Brain Corp; Nilfisk → Brain Corp/Carnegie/LionsBotBrain Corp
8. Hospital logisticsNOST Engineering Aethon, Diligent Robotics, Relay Robotics, Mobile Industrial Robots / MiR, Vecna Robotics—none
9. Retail shelf-scanPARTIALSimbe Robotics, Badger Technologies, Zippedi, Pensa Systems, Trax RetailSam's Club inventory-scanning robots → Brain Corp; Floor-care / multi-purpose OEMs → Brain CorpBrain Corp
10. Security patrolPARTIALKnightscope, Cobalt AI, Robotic Assistance Devices, Asylon Robotics, SMP RoboticsGhost Robotics → Palladyne AI — Robot Report; Ghost Robotics → Onyx Industries — Robot ReportPalladyne AI, Onyx Industries, Levatas
11. Sidewalk deliveryNOStarship Technologies, Serve Robotics, Coco Robotics, Avride, KiwibotMagna → Cartken — Magna manufactures robots that integrate Cartken's AI autonomous-navigation brain; but this is a hardware OEM buying a brain from a vertically-integrated robot maker, not a robot operator buying from a pure-play vendor; Mitsubishi → Cartken — Cartken provides the autonomy stack while Mitsubishi handles local deployment in Japan; again a channel/integrator deal, Cartken is not an independent thinking-layer pure-playnone
12. Road AVs / robotaxisPARTIALWaymo, Tesla, Zoox, Motional, Pony.aiVolkswagen/MOIA → Mobileye — VW's ID. Buzz robotaxi/ride-pooling runs on Mobileye Drive, an independent L4 system; Holon → Mobileye — autonomous shuttle/mover uses Mobileye DriveMobileye, Nuro, Plus
13. AgriculturalPARTIALJohn Deere, Blue River Technology, Bear Flag Robotics, Carbon Robotics, Verdant RoboticsDoosan Bobcat → Agtonomy — Bobcat AT450X autonomous tractor is 'enabled by Agtonomy'; Bobcat embeds Agtonomy's TeleFarmer autonomy software/embedded-compute at the factory rather than building its own brain; Kubota → Agtonomy — Kubota integrates Agtonomy autonomy softwareAgtonomy, Bonsai Robotics
14. ConstructionPARTIALBuilt Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, Pronto.ai, AIM Intelligent Machines, Gravis RoboticsNo major construction OEM/operator is documented buying a pure independent third-party 'brain' to embed — the closest is operators retrofitting via Teleo/SafeAI, but those are full kit+software bundles, not a licensed thinking-layer; Field AI → — markets hardware-agnostic robot foundation models, but construction is one of several verticals and it largely deploys/operates rather than licensing a brain into third-party construction iron with named paying construction customersField AI, Teleo
15. Mining / subseaPARTIALCaterpillar, Komatsu, Sandvik, Oceaneering, SaipemRoy Hill → ASI Mining/Mobius — retrofitted its mixed haul-truck fleet to autonomy using ASI's OEM-agnostic Mobius platform; Heidelberg Materials → Pronto.ai — deploys Pronto's vision-first retrofit autonomous haulage on aggregate/quarry trucks, an independent 3rd-party brainPronto.ai, Greensea IQ, Hexagon
16. HumanoidsPARTIALTesla, Figure AI, 1X Technologies, Sanctuary AI, AgiBotApptronik -> Google DeepMind; Boston Dynamics -> Toyota Research InstitutePI, Skild, NVIDIA GR00T sell to the tail — but thin: PI/Skild are vertically integrating and GR00T is free OSS
17. Quadruped inspectionYESBoston Dynamics, ANYbotics, DEEP Robotics, Unitree Robotics, Ghost RoboticsBoston Dynamics Spot operators → Levatas — license Levatas' Cognitive Inspection Platform as the analytics brain on Spot; Levatas is BD's first Certified Industrial-AI Technology Partner; Spot + mixed-fleet operators → Energy Robotics/Korial — buy an independent full-stack, hardware-agnostic autonomy + analytics OS running across ~8 robot/drone brands ; the brain is third-party, not the OEM'sEnergy Robotics, Levatas
18. Drones / UAVsYESZipline, Skydio, DJI, Wing, AndurilQuantum Systems → Auterion .; Wingcopter → Auterion / PX4 ecosystem .Auterion (Skynode/PX4, licensed to OEMs), DroneDeploy, Pix4D; Shield AI Hivemind (licensable, but Shield AI is also a vertically-integrated incumbent — see the founder section, 'If you built it')
19. Consumer homeNOiRobot, Roborock, Ecovacs Robotics, Dreame Technology, NarwalLong-tail white-label brands → 3irobotix; Smaller brands → Slamtec SLAMWARE + RPLIDARnone
20. Social / elder-careNOBear Robotics, Pudu Robotics, Keenon Robotics, OrionStar, Diligent RoboticsIntuition Robotics — keeps its defensible 'Relationship Orchestration Engine'/personalization in-house but layers multiple model-lab LLMs plus Azure Text-to-Speech for generative conversation — lab-bundled, not an independent robot-brain vendor.; Richtech Robotics — builds the robot/motion itself but integrates Microsoft Azure AI and NVIDIA for vision/voice/reasoning — lab/platform-bundled, not an independent thinking-layer vendor with a moat.none
21. TelepresenceNODouble Robotics, OhmniLabs, Ava Robotics, Blue Ocean Robotics, temi—none
22. ExoskeletonsNOWandercraft, Cyberdyne, Ekso Bionics, ReWalk Robotics, German Bionic—none
23. Defense ground/seaYESAnduril Industries, Milrem Robotics, Saronic, Saildrone, Kraken RoboticsU.S. Army RCV program → Overland AI — awarded an OTA to integrate its vehicle-agnostic OverDrive autonomy software onto government-owned RCV platforms; U.S. Army RCV / ground-vehicle programs → Forterra — supplies platform-agnostic AutoDrive/PATH A-kit autonomy; won Army contract for autonomy retrofit kits on medium tactical vehiclesOverland AI, Forterra, Kodiak Robotics, Applied Intuition
24. Space / planetaryNOGITAI, Intuitive Machines, Astrobotic, Starfish Space, Northrop Grumman SpaceLogistics—none

For the "not yet" segments — the leader's GO / NO-GO gates. A NO/PARTIAL above is the follower's read; most aren't structurally blocked, just early. Each row lists the discrete gates a first-mover watches: ✓ = a GO signal firing as of May 2026 (a reason to go), ✓ = a NO-GO blocker still up (a reason to wait), ✗ = not yet firing / cleared. You go when enough GO gates turn green and the red blockers fall. (Structural NOs — surgery, telepresence, exoskeletons, space, on-device consumer home — have no gate; physics doesn't move.)

SegmentGO gates (✓ firing)NO-GO gates (✓ still blocking)Net read
1 · Fixed industrial arms✓ OEMs partner-for-the-brain — Skild Brain→ABB+UR (Mar 2026)
✓ separable real-time control interface ships (FANUC Stream Motion, ABB EGM, KUKA RSI)
✓ hardware commoditizing — 14 sub-$10K arms; software ~52% of value		✓ safety-cert lockout (ISO 10218:2025) — AI barred from the rated control loop
✓ brain bundled free with hardware (GR00T + OEM house brands)		All 3 GO firing, both blockers up → a vision/supervisory sliver today, not full independent control.
2 · AI pick / pack✓ operators unbundle the pick brain — Ambi AI Skill Suite (Feb 2026); PI licenses π0
✓ VLA generalizes on long-tail SKUs (VLA-as-primary 3→11+ in a year) — early
✗ open manipulation-policy interface mandated — not yet (VDA 5050 = fleet, not grasp)		✓ top buyers keep the brain in-house & absorb winners (Amazon/Covariant, Symbotic)
✓ no separable interface; open weights cap willingness-to-pay		Unbundling firing (Ambi/PI), but no grasp interface + top buyers internalize → only the long tail is addressable.
3 · Food / lab / pharmacy✗ a food-grade VLA hits production reliability — capability rising, not there yet
✗ a food OEM (Marel/Middleby/JBS) licenses an outside brain — firing in industry, not food
✓ hardware commoditizing + labor shock (62% can't fill roles, $25/hr dishwashers)		✓ food-core leaders build in-house — Chef Robotics' own Food Foundation Model (May 2026)
✓ no separable interface (turnkey cell + brain + data sold as one)		Commoditization + labor shock firing; no food VLA at reliability and no OEM defection → position & watch.
4 · Cobots✓ OEMs partner-for-the-brain — UR↔Generalist AI (GTC Mar 2026); UR/MiR+ABB↔Skild
✓ hardware commoditizing — ASP falling; 3 of top-4 cobot brands now Chinese
✓ open programming interface (UR PolyScope X + URCap SDK; NVIDIA Isaac runtime)		✗ OEM-closed-brain OS — UR/ABB are open; only NEURA-style OEMs closed
✓ seats filling fast — Skild ($14B)/PI already signed UR/MiR/ABB; cert favors the partnered		Decisive GO firing → category open. Enter only with a differentiated wedge before the seats fill.
6 · Warehouse AMRs✓ open AMR-native interface — VDA 5050 v3.0.0 (Mar 2026); Seegrid targeting compliance
✓ OEMs partner-for-the-brain on NVIDIA Isaac (KION/Accenture, Cyngn) + HW commoditizing
✓ a hardware-agnostic brain monetizes — Skild bought Zebra's Robotics Automation business (Apr 2026), via M&A not clean licensing		✓ profitable volume stays in-house (Amazon DeepFleet 1M+ robots; Symbotic; Geek+ Brain)
✓ re-cert per OEM body / NVIDIA could give the brain away free		Interface + partners firing, but the biggest volume builds in-house → enter the mixed-fleet / mid-market wedge.
8 · Hospital logistics✓ brain decouples from one body — Serve acquiring Diligent/Moxi, shared AI stack (Jan 2026)
✓ rebuilt on commodity foundation models — Moxi 2.0 on NVIDIA GR00T/Jetson Thor (Oct 2025)
✗ hospital IT mandates an open multi-vendor interface — only early (60% report lock-in pain)		✓ in-house full-stack RaaS is the moat (Aethon/Relay/MiR; Moxi 1.25M+ deliveries of data)
✓ no separable / cert-ready interface (brain fused to elevator/door/EHR/badge)		Decoupling firing, but the brain is consolidating inside a vertically-integrated platform, not sold separately.
9 · Retail shelf-scan✓ leader decouples perception from chassis — Simbe Tally 4.0 ingests robot+fixed+RFID+mobile (Jan 2026)
✓ horizontal foundation layer matures (NVIDIA Isaac GR00T N1.7 commercial licensing)
✓ value → managed-outcome — Brain Corp + Driveline ShelfOptix (Sept 2025)		✓ in-house CV stays the bundled moat (Simbe/Focal/Trax; no ingest API for foreign imagery)
✓ fixed cameras could collapse the robot-brain TAM → only a capture-agnostic brain survives		All 3 GO firing in nascent form, but the in-house-CV moat holds + a fixed-cam threat → opening, not open.
10 · Security patrol✓ hardware commoditizing hard — Unitree Go2 ~$1,600, 30k+ shipped; DroneDog = Spot+payload+SW
✓ OEM partner-for-the-brain — BD certified Levatas on all Spot (single-OEM, early)
✓ labor shock — guard turnover 77%; DroneDog 250k+ missions		✓ leaders keep the brain in-house & bundled (Knightscope "Signals" AI + Mar-2026 guard buys)
✓ outdoor false-alarm bar unmet (~11.7% FP); no cross-OEM open interface yet		Commoditization firing hard, BD/Levatas half-open; leaders bundle in-house + nuisance-alarm bar → not yet open.
11 · Sidewalk delivery✓ hardware commoditizes — Coco 2 = Segway+RoboSense+Jetson; unit $8.5k→$5.2k→<$3k
✓ a full-stack operator buys a brain component — Niantic Spatial VPS into Coco's fleet (Mar 2026)
✗ an aggregator mandates an open autonomy interface — Uber Auto Solutions does NOT (Feb 2026)		✓ full driving policy + data flywheel stays the moat (Serve's 4 acquisitions incl. Diligent)
✓ no mandated interface (cities regulate permits/fees, not control)		HW commoditized + first component unbundled (Niantic VPS); full policy stays the moat → enter at the component layer, not the whole brain.
12 · Road AVs✓ OEMs partner-for-the-brain in trucking/OEM-fleet — Plus/TRATON $25M; Nuro→Lucid/Uber ~20k; Aurora/Volvo
✓ hardware commoditizing — Aurora 2nd-gen kit −50% cost
✓ aggregator opens a driver socket — Uber Autonomous Solutions (Feb 2026), early		✓ dense-urban robotaxi leaders keep the driver in-house (Waymo 1M+ rides; Tesla closed)
✓ urban safety-case / liability can't yet be decoupled from the platform		Firing in trucking + OEM-fleet robotaxi; blocked in dense-urban robotaxi → target OEM/trucking lanes, not urban operators.
13 · Agricultural✓ OEMs partner-for-the-brain — Mach into 25+ OEMs (Dec 2025); Agtonomy in Kubota M5 + Bobcat
✓ a separable perception layer — Deere's retrofittable 16-cam kit (CES 2026), but no open 3rd-party API
✗ a certified 3rd-party autonomy interface — not yet (ISOBUS = implement control only); H-2A wage cut blunts ROI		✓ Deere fences the volume — integrated Blue River + Bear Flag; row-crop in-house, dealer/data-locked
✓ no separable safety interface + cheaper 2026 H-2A labor undercuts capex		Partner-for-brain firing in the specialty/orchard tail; row-crop volume stays in-house → open tail, not the core.
14 · Construction✓ OEM sells a 3rd-party brain via its own channel — Komatsu "Smart Quarry, powered by Pronto" (Aug 2025)
✓ embodiment-agnostic brain + named deployment — FieldAI ($405M/$2B) on a DPR jobsite (Nov 2025)
✗ an open autonomy/control API — only a data schema (AEMP 2.0) exists, not a control socket		✓ Caterpillar keeps the brain in-house, data the moat — 5 in-house autonomous machines (CES 2026)
✓ ASIL-D / liability lockout, no separable control API on new iron		Firing in the retrofit/quarry + non-Cat tail (Komatsu+Pronto, FieldAI); Cat fences its new-machine core → enter the tail.
15 · Mining / subsea✓ OEM stops building, buys the brain — Komatsu + Applied Intuition Vehicle OS (Sept 2025)
✓ brain proven on 3rd-party hardware, paid — Nauticus ToolKITT on a retrofit ROV (Nov 2025); Pronto vision-AHS
✓ a buyer-pulled open-interface standard is emerging — ISO/TS 21815-2 + EMESRT L9 (voluntary, ICMM-driven)		✓ surface AHS consolidating into OEMs by M&A (Cat Command, Epiroc LinkOA; Epiroc bought ASI)
✗ safety-cert lockout — holds underground/Ex, but cleared for subsea IMR (Nauticus shipped)		All 3 GO firing; the open lane is subsea IMR + underground — surface ultra-class haulage is OEM-consolidating, so avoid it head-on.
16 · Humanoids✓ hardware commoditizing — Unitree ASP −70%, sub-$5k bipedal; 12 commercial platforms
✓ long-tail OEMs partner-for-the-brain — Skild via ABB/UR; PI $300/robot/mo; AGIBOT/NEURA/LG on GR00T
✓ open separable interface — ROS 2 + LeRobot + a VLA is the de-facto stack		✓ the frontier keeps the brain as the moat (Tesla, Figure/Helix in-house, 1X)
✓ brain-labs swallow the lane / value stays free (Skild+PI integrating; GR00T free OSS)		3/3 GO firing for the long tail; the frontier builds in-house and free GR00T covers the tail → enter only a humanoid-specific niche.
20 · Social / elder-care✗ a social/elder-care OEM licenses an external brain — proven adjacent (PI, Skild, LingBot), not in-segment yet
✗ a buyer RFP demands grounded, memory-persistent interaction a chat LLM can't do — only early
✓ labor/demand shock — Japan 570k care-worker shortfall by 2040 — but it funds hardware, not the brain		✓ incumbents keep nav/HRI/SLAM as the moat; chat is a free bundled LLM (Pudu PUDU CLOUD; Keenon)
✓ safety-cert lockout (ISO 13482; EU AI Act high-risk from Aug 2026)		Model proven adjacent + labor shock real, but no in-segment OEM defection yet → pre-position; enter on the first OEM defection.

If you built it — the four bets, ranked two ways#

The flat "cloud serving for any robot" company is the no-go from the verdict above — but value does exist in a few vertical niches, and four are worth a serious look. (These four re-cut the proven segments above: the inspection business pulls from both inspection robots and inspection drones; defense splits into a drone play and a ground/maritime play; cleaning stands alone.) There are two honest ways to rank them, and they disagree on purpose: a follower ranks by what's proven and bankable today (existing buyers, contract size, time-to-revenue); a leader ranks by where a funded buyer has just forced the market open — accepting that "no one buys an independent brain yet" is exactly what you'd expect before a category exists. Where the AI runs is a separate axis again — only inspection is genuinely cloud-native, the two defense plays run onboard, cleaning is hybrid; each row is tagged.

A · The follower's ranking — by proven commercial attractiveness. If you want existing-buyer proof before you commit, this is the order, with the economics. Scoring basis (judgment, not a derived score): buyer proof (named paying contracts) · contract size · time-to-revenue / sales cycle · competitive intensity · cloud-fit · window size. Drone (#1) ranks above ground/maritime (#3) on buyer proof, shorter sales cycle, lower clearance burden, and competitive intensity that is fast-follower but not yet winner-take-all — not on contract size, where ground/maritime has the larger named booked dollars (Overland AI $18.6M + $100M raised; Kodiak $49.9M; Applied Intuition $49M + $171M + $249M BPA vs Auterion's ~$50M kit revenue). Both rank MEDIUM-HIGH on attractiveness; the labels collapse to the same bucket because that trade-off is real.

Rank & opportunityICP — who writes the check & why they buyPricing & unit economicsFirst contract & est. ARRComps & key risk
#1 · Drone defense autonomy-OS
EW-survivable terminal-guidance / GPS-denied autonomy licensed to drone OEMs + sold to attritable-drone programs. Attractiveness: HIGH · Runs ONBOARD/edge — real-time, EW/GPS-denied → not cloud-served		Mid-tier Western/allied drone OEMs (20–500 ppl, $5–200M rev; FPV / loitering-munition / Group 1–3 ISR) that build cheap airframes but have no autonomy/EW team — plus DoD program offices (DIU, Army AAL, SOCOM) and Ukraine Brave1 buying autonomy as government-furnished kit. Why buy: airframes are commoditized; jam-resistant terminal autonomy in contested EW is the scarce, hard part. $13.4B+ budget line growing ~35%/yr (total US+allied uncrewed-systems incl. Replicator/Ukraine; the US autonomous-drone line is DoD FY26 ~$9.4B; the software-attributable TAM is ~$1.8B @ ~20% — different scopes, not a conflict).Per-airframe license $800–2,500 (software-on-OEM-compute royalty $300–800) + $250K–1.5M OEM platform license/NRE per design-win + fleet SaaS ~$900–1,200/vehicle/yr. ~85–92% GM software-only (45–60% if you also ship the compute board); infra ~$50–150/vehicle/yr.Pilot/prototype $150K–1M (SBIR Ph II ~$1–2M; DIU OTA $0.5–2M). First annual $750K–3M (per-unit license on a 1,000–5,000-unit batch + NRE). Est ARR/logo $1.5–5M → $8–20M+ (program-of-record). Company ARR: 1Y $0.5–2M · 2Y $5–15M · 3–5Y $40–75M (= the SOM; Y3 ~$20–50M en route).Auterion ($130M Series B, ~$600M+ val; $50M / 33,000 Skynode kits ≈ $1,500/unit; Suite Pro $77/veh/mo), Shield AI Hivemind (~$90M rev, $12.7B val), Anduril Lattice. Risk: very well-capitalized incumbents chase the same buyers → must win a sharp capability wedge (EW survivability), not breadth; ITAR + valley-of-death.
#2 · Industrial inspection autonomy + analytics SaaS
hardware-agnostic, across Spot / ANYmal / DJI-dock fleets. Attractiveness: MEDIUM-HIGH (fastest to revenue) · Runs CLOUD-native ✓ — latency-tolerant analytics		Asset owner running ≥2 inspection robots/drone docks at a large site or multi-site — O&G midstream/downstream, electric-utility substations, hyperscale/colo data centers, mines. Check-writer = Plant Reliability/Integrity Manager or Site Ops Director (opex line, not IT). Why buy: neutral across a mixed fleet; displaces a ~$150K/yr human inspector with under-2-yr ROI; 2026 data-center boom.Per-robot autonomy+fleet seat $12–20K/robot/yr (premium over BD Orbit's ~$5–10K — you add domain AI) + per-site platform $25–60K/site/yr + AI model packs (gauge OCR, thermal, gas-leak, corrosion) $8–25K/yr each = the expansion engine. ~75–85% GM; cloud ~10–18% of rev.Pilot $25–75K (60–90-day PoV, one site/robot, one pack). First annual $60–120K (single site, 1–2 robots, per-site + seats + 1 pack); ~$150K with managed-service. Est ARR/logo Y1 $60–120K → Y2 $200–400K → Y3 $500–900K (best logos $1M+). Company ARR: 1Y $0.5–1M · 2Y $3–5M · 3–5Y $17–35M (= the SOM; Y3 ~$8–15M en route).Energy Robotics/Korial ($13.5M+ Series A; Shell/BP/BASF/Merck; 8 OEMs), Percepto ($120M+ raised), BD Orbit (~$5–10K/robot/yr). Risk: the vendor-neutral seat is already taken (Korial, Percepto) → must out-niche (own data centers or one model pack); BD Orbit / DJI adding native features could foreclose the layer.
#3 · Ground/maritime defense autonomy software
decoupled, platform-agnostic, into Army RCV/ISV & Navy USV via the gov open architecture. Attractiveness: MEDIUM-HIGH (biggest checks, hardest to start lean) · Runs ONBOARD/edge — contested comms, real-time		A DoD program/prototyping office running OTAs — DIU (as Army NGCV/PEO-GCS agent) + Army Robotic Capabilities Office / DEVCOM GVSC; secondary Navy PMS 406, SOCOM. Check-writer = the PM/PdL on RDT&E lines (FY26 RCV RDT&E ~$93M+). Why buy: the gov-owned MOSA reference architecture deliberately decouples autonomy from the chassis so it's competed separately; the Army dropped in-house autonomy → a real software-only seat.(1) NRE/integration OTA $5–50M/program over 18–24 mo — gov funds your R&D. (2) Recurring per-vehicle license ~$40–120K/UGV/yr (mid ~$75K), anchored to Anduril Lattice per-asset pricing. (3) Sustainment/MLOps. ~70–85% GM steady-state, but field-support headcount + IL5/IL6 accredited infra ($0.5–2M/yr/program) → services-heavy early.Wedge $250K–3M (SBIR Ph II ~$1–2M). First prototype OTA $5–20M ceiling / 18–24 mo (~$3–10M/yr). Est ARR/program Y1 ~$3–6M (services-heavy) → Y2 ~$8–15M → Y3 ~$20–40M if it converts toward a program-of-record. Company ARR (recurring): 1Y $3–5M · 2Y $8–20M · 3–5Y $30–60M (= the SOM). Total OTA/contract revenue runs higher + lumpier (services-heavy).Overland AI ($18.6M GVAP OT, $100M raised Feb-2026, ~$140M total), Kodiak ($49.9M GVAP OT), Applied Intuition ($15B val; $49M RCV + $171M production + $249M BPA), Forterra ($238M Series C). Risk: incumbents already hold the exact seats with $100M–$1.5B war chests; revenue hinges on a single slippable/cancelable down-select (RCV isn't a program-of-record yet); clearance + IL5/IL6 + ITAR = hardest to start lean.
#4 · Commercial-cleaning autonomy OS
BrainOS-style autonomy/fleet "brain" licensed to floor-care OEMs. Attractiveness: MEDIUM-HIGH for a robotics+cleaning team / LOW for a generic one · Runs HYBRID — control onboard, fleet/analytics cloud		Mid-large Western cleaning-equipment OEMs ($300M–1.5B rev) lacking a deep autonomy org — Nilfisk, Hako, Minuteman, ICE, Fimap, IPC (Kärcher has since insourced its own stack — a displace-the-DIY target, not greenfield); plus the 5 mega facility-services operators (ABM, ISS, Sodexo, Compass, Aramark). Check-writer = VP Product/CTO sponsored by CEO (strategic make-vs-buy). Tiny ~25-buyer TAM. Why buy: a 30–60-engineer multi-year autonomy effort doesn't fit a mechanical OEM; Gausium (40k units, #1) is eating the category and Brain Corp is drifting to retail data → orphan-risk OEMs want a second source.OEM license $1,500–3,000/robot/yr + one-time NRE $250–750K/OEM; or direct-to-operator hardware-agnostic SaaS $1,200–2,400/robot/yr. ~70–80% GM at scale (50–60% early when remote-ops is human-heavy); price NRE near cost-recovery as a design-win.Co-dev/LOI $100–300K; pilot $250–600K (NRE + per-robot on 5–20 units); first annual production $400–900K (NRE + license on first ~150–400 units of one SKU). Est ARR/logo Y1 $300–600K → Y2 $1.0–2.5M → Y3 $3–7M+ (a Brain-Corp-scale account can top 10k robots = $15–30M ceiling). Company ARR: 1Y $0.5–1.5M · 2Y $3–6M · 3–5Y $10–18M (= the SOM).Brain Corp (the template: 40,000+ AMRs, $182M raised, OEM-licensing; BrainOS Clean 2.0 with Tennant Apr-2026; pivoting to ShelfOptix). Risk: Gausium's vertical integration can undercut the licensed-brain thesis entirely; ~25-buyer TAM (losing 2–3 deals is material); slow capital-heavy 12–18-mo cycles; Brain Corp lock-in / price-cut.

B · The leader's ranking — by first-mover opening (May 2026). The same four, re-ordered by where a buyer with money has forced the socket open. This is the bet, not the safe pick — it tops with a different defense play than the follower's. (These are the four proven businesses; a leader's field is wider — reconciled just below.)

Rank & opportunityThe opening a first-mover bets on (dated 2026 signals)Concrete first moveWindow
#1 · Ground/maritime defense
decoupled autonomy SW · runs onboard		The buyer forced it open: the Army cancelled its Robotic Combat Vehicle award (May 2025) and confirmed its in-house autonomy (RTK) dead (Dec 2025), pivoting to vehicle-agnostic; the Navy cancelled MASC and created a new medium-USV marketplace (its "Golden Fleet" push, Mar 2026); and an Army official named the gap — "the autonomy C2 layer connecting autonomy to NGC2" (Sept 2025). Category-creating moves by the buyer, not follower proof.~18–30 mo to first paid. Enter maritime-first — the Navy's just-created medium-USV marketplace is the freshest open socket. M0–3: hire a DARPA-RACER/RCV-credentialed lead; build to the UMAA architecture + Army IOP day one and attack the named C2-to-NGC2 gap, positioned explicitly against Lattice lock-in, not full-stack against the primes. M2–6: win a no-cost CRADA or SBIR Ph I/II ($250K–1M) via a DIU/SOFWERX CSO = the defense "LOI." M4–9: MVP = standards-native translation/C2 middleware on the reference interface → first OTA.Near-term, ~12–18 mo (authored estimate tied to dated milestones). The MUSV marketplace targets testing by end-FY2026 and first production FY2027; the Army GVAP prototype OTAs (Overland $18.6M, Kodiak $49.9M) are already awarded — incumbents hold those seats. The open socket is the standards-native C2-to-NGC2 layer the Army named as the gap (Sept 2025), and the Navy's fresh marketplace (Mar 2026) which has not yet down-selected. Edge = enter that specific gap before it locks, not the full autonomy stack where incumbents with $100M–$1.5B war chests already compete.
#2 · Drone defense
terminal-guidance brain · runs onboard		The socket exists — the USAF is integrating a government-owned A-GRA across its CCA prototypes (Feb 2026) and flew an in-flight Hivemind↔Lattice autonomy swap; the Blue UAS list moved to DCMA (Dec 2025) and its Framework already lists software/autonomy components separately; and the money landed — ~$54.6B for DAWG in the FY27 request (Apr 2026), with the separate Drone Dominance Program targeting 200k attritable drones by 2027. But the brain is already licensed and swapped at scale (Auterion, The Fourth Law) — a fast-follower race, not virgin territory.M0–3: own the attritable last-500m terminal-guidance layer for sub-$2,000 PBAS-class drones (not the CCA coordination layer — Anduril/Shield AI hold it, A-GRA-funded). Make A-GRA compliance + a Blue UAS software-component listing the two hard gates (the literal keys to the licensing market). Demo passive, GPS-and-comms-independent lock-on on a commodity airframe + sub-$150 Jetson-class module. M3–9: get combat-proven via a Ukraine forward-deployment partner (the Vermeer / Auterion / Fourth-Law path) for dated jamming-survival kill-rate data, then port it into a US PBAS / Gauntlet bid; fund the build with an SBIR Phase II / DIU OTA → first batch license.Open now, ~12 mo (authored estimate, not a program deadline). Anchored to: DAWG FY27 ~$54.6B request (Apr 2026) and Drone Dominance Program 200k-drone target by 2027 — both signal procurement volume pulling forward; the Blue UAS software-component listing and A-GRA CCA integration (Feb 2026) are the concrete gates that close as primes win slots. Edge = A-GRA-compliant + Blue-listed + Ukraine-combat-proven before those slots lock — at the wedge (attritable terminal-guidance), not at the top where Anduril/Shield AI already hold seats at scale.
#3 · Inspection
analytics SaaS · cloud-native ✓		A real capability seam — Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 made Spot autonomous (Apr 2026) and the FAA's pending Part 108 BVLOS rule (final-rule targeted 2026) is opening drone inspection — but it's a commercial opening, not buyer-mandated, and Energy Robotics / Percepto already hold the neutral seat, so the first-mover moat is thin.Wk0–6: lead with utility transmission/substation inspection (rides the FAA's pending Part 108 BVLOS rule (proposed Aug 2025; final-rule targeted 2026)) or hyperscale data centers (fastest-growing greenfield, no legacy lock-in). Don't build a robot — build a thin vendor-neutral ingestion + anomaly-analytics layer that speaks BD Orbit, DJI/Skydio dock, and Unitree APIs and writes to a CMMS (IFS or Maximo). Land 2–3 lighthouse customers on mixed fleets (the pain Energy Robotics validates but hasn't saturated). Wk4–10: 2–3 paid pilots from operators who already own a robot → 60–90-day proof-of-value → annual; add an agentic decision tier as the data moat turns.Open now, 12–18 mo (authored estimate; anchored to FAA Part 108 BVLOS final-rule targeted 2026 for drone inspection, and Gemini Robotics-ER Spot integration Apr 2026 for ground) — but defensibility comes from out-niching one vertical, not from being first, because Energy Robotics / Percepto already hold the vendor-neutral seat at scale.
#4 · Cleaning OS
licensed to OEMs · hybrid		Mostly an incumbent move: Brain Corp just reset the buying criterion (BrainOS Clean 2.0, no route-training, Apr 2026). The leader edge here is the thinnest of the four — there's a 10-year incumbent and the only opening is the OEMs it doesn't serve.~14–18 mo to first paid OEM. M0–3: target Tier-2 / regional floor-care OEMs and large building-service contractors locked out of in-house autonomy by the wage/turnover squeeze (Nilfisk, Comac, Fimap — not Kärcher (self-built) or Tennant (Brain Corp holds it)). Lead with the feature BrainOS Clean 2.0 just made the buying criterion (Apr 2026): zero route-training, deploy in hours, hardware-agnostic retrofit onto commoditized chassis. LOI/paid co-dev via ISSA / Interclean. M3–9: a VLA/perception-first stack (open π0-class) running autonomously on ONE chassis, beating intervention rate. M9–14: supervised pilot, 5–20 robots; M14–18: per-robot license + NRE with a ramp clause.Selective / wait. Only worth it with deep cleaning-domain access to the non-Tennant OEMs.

Why they disagree: the follower's #1 (drone-defense) is the proven-licensing pick — the brain is already sold at scale, bankable but a crowded fast-follower race. The leader's #1 (ground/maritime) is the just-mandated-open pick — riskier and services-heavy early, but where the government killed its in-house option and built the socket at the C2/NGC2 and MUSV layers. The tension is real and intentional: incumbents (Overland, Kodiak, Applied Intuition) already hold the GVAP prototype seats with $100M–$1.5B war chests — those slots are consolidating. The leader's bet is the layer adjacent to those seats (standards-native C2 middleware, the named gap) and the Navy's fresh MUSV marketplace (Mar 2026, not yet down-selected) — position there before that layer locks too. A follower takes drone or inspection; a leader takes ground/maritime — or, from the gates table above, mining/subsea or cobots, where the opening is firing just as hard.

The leader's field is wider than those four. The ranking covers only the four proven businesses — but a first-mover bets wherever the opening is firing, and the gates table flags "not yet" segments firing just as hard: most notably mining/subsea (an emerging open-interface standard — ISO/TS 21815-2 + EMESRT, voluntary — plus an OEM buying the brain) and cobots ("category open": UR↔Generalist + an open URCap interface). Fold both tables together and the leader's first-mover shortlist (May 2026) breaks into three tiers: (1) buyer-mandated — ground/maritime defense, mining/subsea, drone-defense (a government or buyer forced the socket open); (2) OEMs starting to partner-for or de-risk a build — cobots, warehouse AMRs, AV trucking, the construction & ag retrofit tails — though the playbook shows most named players (Standard Bots, Ati, Agtonomy, Bonsai) still build in-house, so the motion is intercept-a-build; (3) proven but contested — inspection, cleaning. This table is the proven-business slice of that shortlist; the gates table is the rest of the leader's field.

One objection hangs over both rankings: those revenue ramps and windows look slow for an AI company in 2026 — wouldn't AI compress them? On the building side, yes — a five-to-ten-person team can now build an autonomy stack that once took fifty engineers, which is partly why a small entrant is viable at all. On the revenue side, no: robots ship at factory speed, safety and aviation certifications run on a regulator's calendar, and defense buys at government pace. The AI-app records (a coding tool hitting $100M in a year) don't transfer to physical, regulated systems. The first year or two of each ramp holds as shown; the upside is in the later tail. Investors do count on the speed — but they price it into valuations (Anduril ~$30B+, Shield AI ~$12.7B), not into revenue curves.

How big is the prize?#

For each of the four proven businesses, the yearly revenue opportunity for the AI-software layer only (not the robots themselves). TAM is the whole market; SAM is what an independent vendor could realistically serve once you remove the wrong geographies and the buyers who build in-house; SOM is what one focused company could win in 3–5 years. Everything is an estimate, built from public market reports and named comparable companies, with 2026 as the base year. (The wider "not yet" leader plays from the gates table — mining, cobots, warehouse, etc. — aren't sized here; they're earlier-stage.)

Opportunity TAM 2026E (top-down × bottom-up) SAM 2026E = TAM × geo × buy-not-build SOM — obtainable ARR: 1Y / 2Y / 3–5Y (defended) Sources / comps
#1 Drone defense autonomy-OS ~$1.8B (2025 ~$1.5B × ~20% CAGR) → ~$3.5–4.5B by 2030. Top-down: addressable platform base ~$16B (loitering-munition $5.4B + Group 1–3/tactical ~$10.5B) × ~10% software-attributable. Bottom-up: ~50 Western OEMs × ~$8–15M + ~25 national programs × ~$30–50M ≈ $1.2–2.0B. The two converge. ~$0.1–0.6B (range). $1.8B × 58% (Western/NATO+allied, exportable; excl. China/Russia/Iran indigenous) = ~$1.0B addressable-geo, then × buy-not-build — three honest scenarios: ceiling 57% (top-down: excl. Anduril/AeroVironment/Elbit who build captive autonomy) → ~$0.6B; mid ~33% (incl. hybrids who rent part of the stack, per the ICP screen) → ~$0.34B; observed-demand floor ~10% (the ~3/30 pure vendor-buyers the bottom-up ICP screen actually finds) → ~$0.1B. The headline/SOM below anchors to the mid/observed case, not the 57% ceiling. 1Y ~$0.5–2M · 2Y ~$5–15M · 3–5Y ~$40–75M (3–5Y ≈ 7–13% of the ceiling SAM, or ~13–25% of the mid/observed SAM — an expected-value range across low/base/high SAM, not reverse-fit to a clean SOM or best-case logo math). Build to 3–5Y: ~5–8 mid-tier OEM design-wins at ~$1.5–5M ARR each (per-unit royalty + platform license + fleet SaaS) + 1–2 government program OTAs (~$10–30M). Defensibility: SAM is fragmented across ~50 OEMs + ~25 programs, so this is a handful of wins, not dominance, with Replicator/Ukraine pulling demand. Comp: ≈ half of Shield AI Hivemind's ~$90M autonomy-software revenue. M&M military-UAV $15.8B→$22.8B; M&M loitering-munition $5.36B @19.9%; M&M counter-UAS $6.64B @25.1%; DoD FY26 ~$9.4B autonomous-drone; Shield AI Hivemind ~$90M, Anduril Lattice.
#2 Inspection autonomy + analytics SaaS ~$1.8B (2025 ~$1.6B × ~15% CAGR) for the 4 verticals; ~$3–4B all-vertical. Top-down: FMI drone-inspection $15.2B × 47.6% SW → haircut to recurring autonomy+analytics SaaS (~40%) ≈ $2.9B × ~52% (O&G + utilities + data-centers + mining) + ground-robot inspection SW. Bottom-up (penetration curve, not all-addressable-now): the full ~20,000–30,000-site universe is the fully-penetrated TAM at ~$60–90K each ≈ $1.5–2.4B — but only ~2,200 sites deploy a robot/drone today (<10% penetration), so the near-term served market is ~2,200 × ~$60K ≈ ~$0.13B now, growing along the penetration curve toward the $1.8B TAM as adoption climbs. Do not read the 20–30k-site figure as addressable today. ~$0.1–0.5B (range). $1.8B × 68% (Western/allied geo; excl. China/sanctioned where DJI/domestic dominate) = ~$1.2B addressable-geo, then × buy-not-build — three scenarios: ceiling ~40% (top-down: excl. super-majors/hyperscalers building in-house + OEM-captive) → ~$0.5B; mid ~33% (incl. hybrids) → ~$0.4B; observed-demand floor ~10% (pure vendor-buyers from the ICP screen) → ~$0.12B. SOM below anchors to the mid/observed case. 1Y ~$0.5–1M · 2Y ~$3–5M · 3–5Y ~$17–35M (3–5Y ≈ 4–7% of the ceiling SAM, or ~14–29% of the observed-floor SAM — treating the geo/buy-not-build multipliers as low/base/high estimates, this is an expected-value range, not best-case logo math). Build to 3–5Y: reconciled to per-logo ARR — at the ranking table's Y3 ~$0.5–0.9M/logo, $17–35M of company ARR is ~20–50 multi-site logos (not 120–200; the earlier site-count math double-counted sites already inside multi-site logos). Each logo spans ~5–15 paying program-sites at ~$70–90K blended (per-site + seats + 1–2 model packs). Defensibility: lower share than #1 because the vendor-neutral seat is already held (Korial, Percepto) → you must out-niche one vertical (data centers). Comp: ≈ Percepto's ~$26M ARR (2024 category leader) — roughly matching today's leader in 3–5 yr. FMI drone-inspection $15.2B (47.6% SW); M&M $11.6B→$23B @14.6% (NA ~40%); inspection-robots ~$6.7B; Energy Robotics/Korial, Percepto (~$26M ARR), BD Orbit ($5–10K/robot/yr).
#3 Ground/maritime defense autonomy SW ~$1.1B (2025–26 ~$1.0B, +~8–10%) → ~$2.5–3B by 2030. Top-down: military UGV ~$2.5B + military USV/UUV ~$1.36B ≈ $3.9B platform; the autonomy thinking-layer software is a slice of that. We show both methods: (a) the same ~9–15% software-attributable haircut used for drones gives ~$0.4–0.6B; (b) anchoring directly to the DoD FY26 ~$1.2B cross-platform autonomy-SW budget line rolled to 2026E gives ~$1.1B (≈28% of the $3.9B platform base). The 2–3× gap is real and method-driven, not a fact — defense plausibly carries a higher autonomy-SW share than airframe-heavy drones (C2/mission-autonomy is a larger fraction of a UGV/USV's value than of a $2k loitering munition), but that premium is an assumption, so the honest TAM band is ~$0.4–1.1B and the SAM/SOM below use the ~$1.1B budget-anchored case as the high end. ~$0.1–0.4B (range). $1.1B × 85% (US + Five-Eyes/NATO + Israel/Korea; China/Russia non-addressable) = ~$0.94B addressable-geo, then × buy-not-build — three scenarios: ceiling ~45% (top-down: decoupled MOSA/OTA only; excl. Army in-house RTK + prime-locked autonomy) → ~$0.4B; mid ~33% (incl. hybrids) → ~$0.31B; observed-demand floor ~10% → ~$0.09B. SOM below anchors to the mid/observed case. 1Y ~$3–5M · 2Y ~$8–20M · 3–5Y ~$30–60M recurring (3–5Y ≈ 8–15% of the ceiling SAM and higher against the mid/floor SAM; an expected-value range across low/base/high multipliers, not best-case logo math; total OTA/contract revenue is higher + lumpier). Build to 3–5Y: win 2–4 program lanes — an ISV-class OTA (~$5M/yr) ramping to an $18.6M-class prototype + per-vehicle licenses + sustainment; mostly OTA/services with a growing recurring tail. Defensibility: defense SAM is concentrated in a few programs, so 8–15% = winning 2–4 of them; single-down-select risk widens the range. Comp: Overland AI ($18.6M GVAP OT, $100M raised Feb-2026, ~$140M total) / Kodiak ($49.9M GVAP OT) trajectory. Mordor military-UGV $1.96B→$3.08B @7.8%; Unmanned-Sea-Systems $2.62B (defense 52%); DoD FY26 ~$1.2B autonomy SW; Overland AI, Kodiak, Forterra, Applied Intuition.
#4 Commercial-cleaning autonomy OS ~$0.45B (2025 ~$0.40B × ~11.7% CAGR; range $0.39–0.54B). Top-down: robotic floor-scrubber market ~$1.2–1.3B + co-deployed autonomous vacuums → ~$1.8–2.0B pro base × ~20% autonomy/perception/fleet-SW share.
