Bottom-up: global installed robotic-scrubber base × ~$1.5–3K/robot/yr software. ~$0.03–0.12B (range). $0.45B × 60% (Western/allied; China's Pudu/Gausium closed) = ~$0.27B addressable-geo, then × buy-not-build — three scenarios: ceiling ~45% (top-down: legacy OEMs; excl. vertically-integrated) → ~$0.12B; mid ~33% (incl. hybrids) → ~$0.09B; observed-demand floor ~10% → ~$0.03B — and Brain Corp already holds the Tennant-locked chunk, so a new entrant's pool is smaller still. SOM below anchors to the mid/observed case. 1Y ~$0.5–1.5M · 2Y ~$3–6M · 3–5Y ~$10–18M (3–5Y ≈ 8–15% of the ceiling SAM and higher against the mid/floor SAM; an expected-value range across low/base/high multipliers, not best-case logo math). Build to 3–5Y: win 2–4 mid-tier / orphan-risk Western OEMs; each logo ramps ~$1–2.5M (Y2) → ~$3–7M (Y3) on per-robot licenses as their fleet ships → blended ~$10–18M by Y3–5. Defensibility: smallest SOM — a ~25-buyer TAM, Gausium price pressure, and Brain Corp lock-in cap it; 8–15% = a few second-source design-wins. Comp: Brain Corp built >40k robots over ~a decade ($182M raised). IndustryResearch commercial-cleaning-robots ~$6.85B; openPR/Coherent robotic-floor-scrubber $1.1B→$2.64B @11.7% (NA 36%; Tennant 24.5%, Nilfisk 20.1%); Brain Corp.

Even the largest is a ~$1.8B software market — niche, not a mega-market. That is the point: this is a real but narrow opportunity — and narrower still in practice: the per-company ICP screen below finds most even well-matched buyers build in-house today (only ~3 of 30 are pure vendor-buyers). So the SAM buy-not-build fractions are a top-down ceiling on the dollar pool, and the serviceable slice is gated on intercepting or de-risking a build, not on buyers shopping to rent.

Who would actually buy it? — the ICP shortlist#

Method. We screened all 2,584 companies in the directory (3,074 placements across segments/regions) — each tagged with what it does, funding, stage and region — segment by segment, against one question: would this company rent a cloud thinking layer, or build its own? This is a best-effort full-census screen across the directory as constructed, not a hand-picked sample. But the directory itself was built from public sources (Crunchbase, company sites, industry databases) with standard deduplication, so coverage is broad but not provably exhaustive — smaller or stealth-mode companies are likely undercounted. The ideal customer is a Seed-to-Series-C robot OEM or operator with a credible body but no frontier research org, selling into a latency-tolerant, non-regulated domain where the 1-10 Hz reasoning layer is the differentiator they must ship fast — and would rather rent than build. (In reality — see the acquisition playbook below — 20 of the 30 are building in-house today; this screen flags who has the strongest economic reason to rent, and the playbook is how we’d actually convert that.)

The ICP is a "rent-the-brain" robot company, not a brain-builder — but in practice most build anyway (see the acquisition playbook below), so the real target is a builder you intercept, not a shopper. Concretely: a hardware-first OEM or a robot-as-a-service operator whose value proposition is the physical machine, the deployment, or the service contract — but whose competitive gap is autonomy. They have enough capital to pay for a usage-priced cloud reasoning layer, but not the budget to staff a frontier VLA/foundation-model team — yet the screen below shows most build some autonomy in-house anyway. So the sweet spot is not "can pay, won't build"; it is "can pay, and currently DOES build — the intercept target": a funded team already staffing an autonomy effort where the bet is that renting the 1-10 Hz reasoning layer is cheaper or faster than continuing that build. That renting-beats-building claim is the thesis, and it is unproven (see H12 / the playbook).

Funding band (a heuristic, not a law): roughly $5M-$150M, concentrated in Seed through Series C, is where the economic case is densest — but it is a tendency, not a rule. Below ~$2M (e.g. Eliport at $0.16M, Ubica at $0.76M) the fit is structurally right but the company usually cannot pay — though some tiny firms still buy a narrow long-tail layer. Above ~$200-300M (Dexterity $300M, Symbotic, Figure, Skild) the company has the cash to build its own stack and usually does, though a well-funded firm can still choose to buy an adjacent layer. The densest pockets sit at Seed-Series B in the $10-90M range. Caveat: ability-to-pay is unvalidated for the ~40-45% of named targets with no disclosed funding — for them the "can pay" half of the thesis is inferred from stage/segment, not confirmed.

Stage trigger: the buyer is in scaling mode under speed-to-autonomy pressure — racing to expand SKU/task coverage, site count, or fleet density faster than an in-house research hire could deliver. The decisive signal is architecture: they already run hybrid or teleop-assisted control today — transitional postures inside otherwise build-heavy players (Agtonomy's tele-assist, Reflex Robotics'/temi's teleop-to-autonomy, Coco's operator-assist, ICE Cobotics' subscription RaaS) — so cloud/hybrid cognition fits their existing posture rather than requiring a re-architecture.

Domain trigger: the workload must be latency-tolerant and non-safety-critical at the reasoning tier — coverage/route planning, grasp-and-sequence reasoning, social/conversational dialog, planogram diffing, mission orchestration — running at 1-10 Hz while hard-real-time control stays onboard. The single cleanest proof point is commercial cleaning, where Brain Corp's BrainOS already licenses a thinking layer to Tennant, Nilfisk, SoftBank, ICE and RPS — demonstrating the buy-not-build behavior is real and repeatable.

The shortlist — ranked targets. Pulled from the per-segment screens; funding, stage and HQ are the researched figures. These are leads to qualify, not closed accounts.

CompanySegmentFundingStageHQWhy they’d rent, not build
Tier 1 — Beachhead (proven buy-not-build; cleanest cloud-fit) Commercial cleaning and social/service/elder-care are the two strong-rated segments where the buy-not-build behavior is already proven (BrainOS licensees) or structurally inevitable (the differentiator is cloud-native dialog/coverage reasoning). Land here first: these buyers have already chosen to rent autonomy, or their product literally cannot ship without a rented reasoning layer. (LionsBot below is the exception — it is a competitor/partner counted only as a wedge target, not as buyer-proof.)
Tennant Company7. Commercial cleaning—publicUSAAlready a proven rent-the-brain buyer running BrainOS on its autonomous line; a hardware-first floor-machine OEM that has explicitly chosen to license autonomy. Not a clean winnable beachhead: Brain Corp holds a fresh 3-year exclusive plus a $32M equity stake and board seat — a direct displacement attempt is unlikely to succeed. The realistic wedge is new form factors or outdoor/multi-surface lines Brain Corp does not currently cover (see the playbook).
Nilfisk7. Commercial cleaning—publicDenmarkClassic floor-machine OEM that partnered with Brain Corp for its autonomous SC50; proven willingness to license a brain rather than build it.
ICE Cobotics7. Commercial cleaning—privateUSACobi runs on BrainOS and the company sells equipment-as-a-subscription, so usage-priced cloud cognition matches its business model exactly.
Kärcher (KIRA)7. Commercial cleaning—private/largeGermanyCleaning-machine giant with strong hardware but previously licensed BrainOS. Not a clean winnable beachhead: Kärcher has since built its own internal autonomy stack (self-built KIRA), making it a displace-the-DIY target rather than a greenfield buyer — harder and slower than approaching OEMs that still lack an autonomy org entirely.
Rice Robotics20. Social / service / elder-care$7MseedHong KongSeed delivery+companion robot maker with no research org, racing to add richer interaction — the differentiator is cloud-friendly dialog/planning, a prime rent-the-brain profile.
Andromeda Robotics (Abi)20. Social / service / elder-care$18MSeries AAustraliaSeries A companion robot whose core product is empathetic conversation/social reasoning — exactly the cloud thinking layer they'd rent rather than build.
Enchanted Tools (Mirokaï)20. Social / service / elder-care$18MseedFranceCharacter/social humanoid where the brain IS dialog + expressive reasoning (cloud-native); seed funding can pay but can't fund a frontier research org.
LionsBot International7. Commercial cleaning$38MSeries ASingaporeCompetitor / partner, wedge-only — NOT buyer-proof. By this doc's own rule (autonomy/perception vendors are competitors, not customers) LionsBot does not count as buy-not-build evidence: it builds its own perception/SLAM + LionsCloud and licenses that brain to others (e.g. Nilfisk). It is listed here solely as a possible wedge target for the under-invested cross-fleet/cloud reasoning layer (see the playbook), never as proof that buyers rent.
Tier 2 — Core vein (moderate segments, strong cloud-fit, sweet-spot funding) The dense, repeatable hunting ground: Seed-Series C OEMs/operators in latency-tolerant manipulation, mobile-robot orchestration, construction task robots, and connected-niche agriculture. These are the textbook 'pay but don't build' companies — funded enough to be real customers, too small for a research org, with workloads that map cleanly to a 1-10 Hz cloud/hybrid brain.
Anyware Robotics3. Stationary latency-tolerant / pick-pack$17MseedUSASeed hardware-first truck/container-unloading robot; far too small to build a frontier grasp/sequence brain, so a usage-priced cloud layer directly accelerates time-to-autonomy.
Contoro Robotics3. Stationary latency-tolerant$22MSeries AUSASeries A unloading operator in the exact funding sweet spot; needs mixed-carton manipulation reasoning it cannot afford to build — prime rent-the-brain candidate.
Chef Robotics2/3. AI pick-pack / food assembly$66MSeries AUSASeries A food-assembly RaaS operator handling deformable, variable ingredients (real latency-tolerant manipulation reasoning) without a frontier research org; renting speeds menu/SKU expansion.
Pickle Robot3/6. Truck unloading$87MSeries BUSASeries B operator with a narrow unstructured-unload use case; speed-to-throughput beats owning frontier autonomy, sweet-spot funding to pay without building a VLA.
Standard Bots4. Cobots$63MSeries BUSASeries B no-code cobot challenger racing to ship autonomy on its own arm without a research org; the AI task-reasoning layer is exactly what it could rent to move faster.
Agtonomy13. Agricultural / field$64MSeries BUSATele-assist/supervised-autonomy model is inherently hybrid-cloud and connectivity-tolerant; Series B operator racing to ship across OEM partners without a frontier research org.
Dusty Robotics14. Construction$69MSeries BUSAScaling Series B layout-robot OEM; BIM-to-floor layout is classic 1-10 Hz thinking-layer work that tolerates cloud/hybrid, with no research org and speed-to-feature pressure.
Onward Robotics6. Warehouse AMRs$20MSeries AUSASeries A AMR+orchestration vendor small enough that building proprietary fleet/path-planning is a distraction, large enough to pay — speed pressure favors renting the brain.
Ati Motors6. Warehouse AMRs$37MSeries BIndiaSeries B industrial AMR OEM scaling globally with no research org; a cloud brain accelerates multi-site fleet reasoning without hiring a planning team.
Bonsai Robotics13. Agricultural / field$15MSeries AUSASeries A retrofit-onto-existing-machinery model in connected orchards (workable hybrid connectivity); classic buy-the-brain sweet spot.
Canvas14. Construction$43MSeries BUSASeries B drywall-finishing OEM in scaling phase; surface-mapping/finish-path planning is cloud-friendly, funded to pay but too small for a planning research org.
Teleo14. Construction$30MSeries AUSASupervised-autonomy retrofit model is explicitly remote/hybrid, so a cloud planning layer fits its architecture; Series A with no frontier research org, racing to ship.
Tier 3 — Opportunistic (right profile, dampened by funding, geography, or partial overlap) Real buy-not-build logic but with a discount: undercapitalized seed players (strong intent, thin ability to pay), connected-but-cloud-friction geographies (China data-residency), or companies that build some autonomy in-house and would only rent a supplementary layer. Pursue selectively as the model proves out.
Persona AI16. Humanoids$42MseedUSASeed industrial humanoid OEM with a narrow high-value task domain and no research org; classic buy-not-build racing to ship on a tight budget.
Reflex Robotics16. Humanoids$7MseedUSATeleop-first wheeled mobile manipulator (non-locomotion-locked) — a natural fit to graduate teleop into cloud-assisted autonomy; tiny seed budget rules out in-house research.
Ascento10. Security patrol$4MseedSwitzerlandSeed hardware-first outdoor patrol OEM with no research org; classic rent-to-ship-faster profile, capped only by the customer's cloud/on-prem security objection.
Zippedi9. Retail shelf-scan$23MSeries A/BChileRaaS shelf-scan operator in the funding sweet spot racing to scale across LatAm/US; slow aisle nav + planogram reasoning are cloud/hybrid-friendly.
Flytrex18. Drones / UAVs$60MSeries CIsraelScaling suburban delivery operator focused on logistics/ops, not autonomy research; connected fleet where cloud/hybrid dispatch and route planning fit.
Percepto18. Drones / UAVs$134MSeries CUSADrone-in-a-box inspection is inherently connected and cloud-backhauled; mission planning/re-tasking at 1-10 Hz fits hybrid, focused on industrial workflows not foundational autonomy.
Keybotic17. Quadruped inspection$3Macquired (small)SpainTiny pure inspection-quadruped operator with zero capacity to build a planning/anomaly brain; renting a cloud layer is the only realistic path to scale autonomy.
Taurob17. Quadruped inspection—acquiredAustriaATEX-certified hazardous-area inspection hardware specialist whose differentiator is the rugged certified platform, not the planning brain — a strong rent-the-brain candidate.
Path Robotics1. Fixed industrial arms$271MSeries DUSAEntire value prop is weld-seam reasoning/path planning layered on third-party FANUC arms; borderline given its scale, but a stronger cloud planning brain could accelerate roadmap.
Yarbo19. Consumer home (commercial/outdoor edge)$30MSeries BUSAHardware-first modular outdoor-yard platform at Series B with hard multi-module RTK/vision planning and no frontier research org; speed-to-autonomy pressure favors renting.

The acquisition playbook — what each target is doing today, and how we’d win them. The 30 above passed the should-they-rent screen on economics. But screening their actual stacks (web research, May 2026) flips the picture, and that reframes the whole go-to-market:

Across the 30 targets the split is 20 building-in-house, 3 pure buy-from-vendor, 7 hybrid-partial, and 0 pure teleop-only or greenfield-none. The buyers are the three cleaning-OEM incumbents (Tennant, Nilfisk, ICE Cobotics) all renting Brain Corp / Carnegie / LionsBot brains. The hybrids (Karcher, Rice, Andromeda, Enchanted Tools, Onward, Reflex, Taurob) buy part of the stack — usually third-party LLMs/VLMs or a perception engine — while hand-building the orchestration/dialogue/fleet layer. Teleop is never a standalone posture; it shows up as a transitional state inside build/hybrid players racing toward autonomy (Reflex on GXO, Teleo, Contoro's InteleOp). The implication for GTM: this is overwhelmingly a build-heavy market, so the dominant motion is not "sell to people shopping for a brain" but "intercept or de-risk a build." Only ~3 targets are actively in a buying posture you can win head-to-head, ~7 are warm because they already rent something, and ~20 require a wedge that explicitly does not touch their core moat. Sell against the cost and opportunity-cost of staffing a frontier ML org, not against a competing product.

Our unique value proposition.

A managed, hosted cloud reasoning brain for robots — the "thinking layer" you rent instead of staff. It runs the 1-10 Hz task-level planning, decision, dialogue and fleet-orchestration that sits above a robot's edge perception/control, hybrid edge/cloud and embodiment-agnostic, so any robotics company ships smarter autonomy and new coverage in quarters — without building, funding, or maintaining a frontier-model research org. Keep your edge nav and your moat; rent the brain you can't afford to staff, and redirect scarce ML headcount to hardware, data and GTM. fal-for-robotics: cognition as a service, billed per robot or per decision, not per research hire.

The three plays — matched to where the target is at.

Play 1 — Abandon-build (vs in-house builders, ~20 targets). This is the unproven core of the GTM — no funded robotics team has a cited precedent of dropping an in-progress build to rent, and switching costs plus the "we own our AI" investor narrative push the other way; it is the central bet to validate, not a proven motion. Core argument: a seed/Series-A/B hardware team cannot out-spend Physical Intelligence / NVIDIA on a foundation-model research org, so renting the 1-10 Hz reasoning brain ships autonomy quarters faster and redeploys scarce ML headcount onto the true moat (hardware, actuation, data, GTM). Never attack the core model — wedge the adjacent, un-staffed layer. Strongest proofs: Standard Bots ("reframe their foundation-model build as a capital sink they can't out-spend... refocus team on arm, data moat, GTM"); Reflex Robotics ("intercept the build before the two Principal-AI hires harden into a sunk-cost research org" — staffing a world-models+RL+sim org runs roughly $0.5-1.5M/yr per 2-4 engineers, escalating toward several million per year as the team grows, versus renting); Chef Robotics and Pickle and Anyware (wedge the long-tail / cold-start SKUs the owned model hasn't learned yet — "ship Day 1, fall back to your model as it catches up"); Dusty and Agtonomy ("rent the planning brain instead of hiring 4 ML engineers you can't afford at Series B").

Play 2 — Displace-incumbent (vs vendor buyers + fragmented hybrids, ~6 targets — Tennant, Nilfisk, ICE, plus hybrids Kärcher, Onward and Taurob). Core argument: per-robot royalties to a single OEM-locked vendor are a fixed tax that caps RaaS margin, hands your roadmap to a supplier, and leaves you with no cross-SKU fleet brain. Offer cheaper per-robot cognition, your own data/roadmap, and one layer across every SKU. Strongest proofs: ICE Cobotics ("Brain Corp royalty is a fixed per-robot tax on every RaaS unit... own your robots' brain, not rent Brain Corp's"); Nilfisk (displace three vendors — Brain Corp, Carnegie, LionsBot — with "one cloud brain across every SKU you control, not them"); Taurob (displace Energy Robotics/Kalypso as the reasoning layer); Tennant is the hard variant — don't fight the fresh 3-yr Brain Corp exclusive head-on, wedge outdoor/multi-surface/new-form-factor as a single-vendor-lock-in hedge on their $250M bet.

Play 3 — Land-greenfield (vs teleop-today / brand-new-orchestration ambitions, ~4 targets). Core argument: target the capability they have publicly committed to but demonstrably cannot staff — the brand-new orchestration/dialogue layer or the teleop-to-autonomy ramp — and become the brain before they sink money into an unstaffable build. Strongest proofs: Ati Motors (the freshly announced "Ati Flow" multi-vendor orchestration brain — "ship it now, not in a 2-yr platform build"); Rice Robotics (the token-funded "AGI Foundry" is "a multi-year, unstaffable money pit" — rent a production dialog+planning brain to hit the Q3-2026 M1 launch); Reflex and Teleo ("turn supervised-teleop hours into autonomous tasks fleet-wide, without a research org").

Pricing-model menu.

These are MODELED ACVs, not quotes. Every figure below ($10–90/robot/mo, 15–25% of a RaaS hour, % of RaaS contract, per-target $120–400k/yr) is fleet size × an assumed rate — derived from comps and unit economics, not discovered budgets or buyer-validated prices, and pending direct discovery. Two cautions: (1) the per-robot/mo figures are a top-down willingness-to-pay anchor that must still clear GPU cost-to-serve — at low fleet counts or heavy 1–10 Hz reasoning load, $10–20/robot/mo may not cover hosting+optimization margin, so it is a floor to validate, not a guaranteed price; (2) all per-target ACV bands are deal-size estimates, not signed ACVs.

Five models, matched to how the target already bills and where the value lands:

ModelIndicativeWhen it fits (records)
Per-robot / month~$10-90/robot/mo ($50-90 for orchestration; $10-20 at huge fleets)Default when the target resells autonomy as MRR and wants a clean line item. Tennant (Clean360 mirror), LionsBot ($10-20 at 5,000 robots), Ati Motors ($50-90 orchestration tier), Karcher, Agtonomy.
Per-robot-hour (or % of RaaS hour)~$0.50-1.00/robot-hr, i.e. 15-25% of a ~$5/hr RaaS rateWhen the customer bills hourly RaaS and value is engagement-time-denominated. Standard Bots ($5/hr RaaS), Andromeda (3,500 hrs logged), Reflex (blended w/ platform fee), Canvas.
Per-decision / API callmetered on escalations / hard picks / re-tasksWhen you complement (not replace) an owned edge model and only fire on the long tail. Anyware (escalated edge-case picks), Chef (uncovered-SKU cold-start), Pickle, Percepto (anomaly-triggered re-tasks).
% of RaaS contract~5-15% of per-robot/monthWhen cognition cost is already embedded as a margin pass-through in their RaaS pricing. Onward Robotics (5-15%, RGo precedent), ICE Cobotics (swaps the Brain Corp royalty 1:1).
Platform fee + usagelow floor + metered (GPU-hours / per-decision / per-token-equiv)Cash-tight seed firms (low floor) and build-heavy players who'll only rent undifferentiated compute. Rice (token-constrained), Enchanted Tools, Persona AI, Bonsai, Dusty, Path Robotics (GPU-hours for world-model sim).

How much would they pay — anchoring willingness-to-pay.

Anchor WTP to the four things buyers can already price: (1) ML labor replaced — the "cheaper than 2-3 loaded ML FTEs (~$400-600k+/yr)" frame that recurs in Enchanted Tools, Ascento, Dusty, Agtonomy; (2) throughput / SLA recovered — "even 1-2% throughput recovery on stalled picks pays the layer" (Anyware, Pickle, Contoro); (3) deployment / time-to-coverage speed — shipping a launch or new SKU/site quarters faster (Rice Q3-2026, Ati Flow, Standard Bots); and (4) a slice of robot revenue — 5-25% of per-robot RaaS economics, or directly undercutting an incumbent royalty (Onward 5-15%, ICE Cobotics, Standard Bots 15-25%). Realistic band by stage: seed / pilot (single-digit to low-tens of units: Rice, Reflex, Persona, Enchanted, Ascento, Andromeda) lands $40-150k/yr, often a $50-120k pilot; Series A-B early-commercial (tens to low-hundreds of units: Contoro, Dusty, Agtonomy, Zippedi, Teleo, Ati, Percepto, Taurob) lands $120-400k/yr; scaled / multi-fleet (hundreds to thousands of robots: LionsBot $600k-1.2M, Pickle $300-600k→$1.5-3M on UPS ramp, Tennant $1.5-3M+, Nilfisk, Flytrex, Yarbo) reaches $600k-3M+/yr. Hard cap to respect: pure builders will never pay for their core loop (Chef's FFM, Path's Obsidian, Bonsai's VLA) — price only the adjacent/burst/long-tail layer, and discount deeply where ownership just closed the door (Keybotic post-Helsing, Tennant's fresh exclusive).

Target by target. Grouped by what they run today, so the play maps to their posture. Every "Willing to pay" figure below is modeled, not quoted — a reasoned deal-size band tied to fleet size, funding and segment economics, derived from desk research rather than any customer conversation or stated budget. Treat each as a hypothesis to test in discovery, not a price.

CompanyDoing todayThe play (how we win them)Pain we solveWilling to payPricing model
Building their own brain in-house · 20
LionsBot InternationalBuilds own perception/SLAM/nav + LionsCloud fleet platform; 13 open autonomy roles, dedicated Autonomy team.They won't abandon core autonomy. Wedge on the cloud/fleet reasoning layer they under-invest in: rent cross-fleet planning/decision intelligence per-robot so their team focuses scarce SLAM/perception talent on hardware-tied work.Autonomy team stretched thin across 5,000+ robots in 30 countries; every new edge case (1-10Hz replanning, multi-robot coordination) competes for scarce SLAM/perception hires.~$600k-1.2M/yr at current 5,000-robot fleet ($10-20/robot/mo), scaling toward ~$2M as fleet grows; framed against ~$3-5M in-house autonomy R&D burn and ~$78M RaaS run-rate.Per-robot/month ($10-20) — mirrors their own RaaS billing so it slots as a clean line item against ~$15.6k/robot/yr revenue, making margin math trivial; usage/decision-API tier as fallback for spiky reasoning load.
Anyware RoboticsBuilds proprietary embodied-AI perception + learning-based motion planning in sim; PhD founders, hiring perception/planning engs.Don't fight their core grasp brain; wedge the long tail. Sell cloud reasoning for novel/damaged/shifted/mixed-SKU loads their sim-trained model fails on, framed as recovery layer that protects throughput SLAs without scaling a research team.Edge-case picks (shifted, damaged, dense mixed-SKU loads) cause stalls/human takeovers; a seed team can't staff a frontier brain for the full long tail.~$40-90k/yr now at a small deployed fleet (single-digit to ~20 robots across early 3PLs); scales to ~$250-400k/yr as fleet hits 50-100+ robots. Justified by 60% labor savings and ~45k-container 3PL volume — even 1-2% throughput recovery on stalled picks pays the layer.Per-decision / API call on escalated edge-case picks, plus a low per-robot/month platform floor. Why: charges only when their own model defers, so it complements rather than replaces their proprietary brain and scales with fleet + container volume.
Contoro RoboticsBuilds own perception + motion/grasp planning (AdaptAI) + teleop HITL data engine (InteleOp); hiring autonomy & perception engineers.Don't fight their teleop edge — replace the expensive in-house planning/perception R&D. Show TCO of a 2-vendor autonomy team vs rented brain; let them redeploy headcount to gripper/GTM and scale carton coverage faster.Mixed-carton grasp/motion reasoning and per-customer model retraining burn scarce Series A engineering payroll and slow new-site coverage.~$120-250k/yr now (est. 15-30 robots across pilot sites at ~$8-15k/robot/yr brain slice); scales to ~$500-800k/yr as fleet approaches ~100 robots on the funding runway.Per-robot/month platform fee + per-decision/API usage for the planning calls. Fits because robots are stationary, latency-tolerant (1-10 Hz), so cloud reasoning is metered per unload cycle; predictable base aligns with their RaaS billing while usage scales with throughput.
Chef RoboticsBuilds proprietary "ChefOS" + "Food Foundation Model"; own perception/manipulation ML; hiring autonomy/perception teamDon't fight their FFM (it's their moat). Wedge in adjacent reasoning: cloud burst for new bi-manual/new-SKU coverage before FFM generalizes, plus sim/synthetic-data + task-orchestration — keep their scarce ML team on the moat, not infra.Scarce ML team must both advance the FFM moat AND hand-cover every new SKU/customer/form-factor; cold-start coverage gaps throttle deployment speed and burn the data flywheel's lead time.Low six figures, expanding. ~$65.6M raised, dozens-to-low-hundreds of robot stations across 12+ facilities. Land ~$60-120k/yr (new-SKU cold-start + sim/data tooling); scales to ~$300-500k/yr as bi-manual prep-table fleet grows. Capped by build-vs-buy: they will not pay for their core loop.Per-decision/API for cloud burst reasoning on uncovered SKUs + platform fee for sim/synthetic-data tooling. Why: aligns to their incremental pain (new-SKU cold-start), avoids per-robot pricing that competes head-on with the FFM they refuse to outsource, and scales with menu/SKU expansion not headcount.
Pickle RobotBuilds own "Physical AI" perception+autonomy stack; fine-tunes foundation models/VLMs in-house; actively hiring perception ML team.Wedge on their own framing: they fine-tune, not pretrain. Rent a hosted reasoning brain for the long-tail rare-event layer so their ML hires ship coverage faster, not staff a frontier team.Long-tail rare events (novel/damaged/occluded freight) break detectors; each new SKU/customer needs costly in-house retraining, slowing per-site rollout and UPS-scale coverage.$300-600k/yr near-term (their growing fleet, ~30+ robots H1'25 plus UPS 400-robot ramp 2026-27); scales to $1.5-3M/yr as fleet crosses several hundred robots. Anchored against ~$300k/robot economics and a multi-FTE perception team they'd otherwise expand.Per-robot/month platform fee plus per-decision usage on the cloud reasoning calls. Fits because robots run high-throughput cycles (1,500+ boxes/hr) where edge handles routine motion and only hard/long-tail decisions hit the cloud brain — usage tracks the value moments, platform fee tracks fleet size.
Standard BotsBuilding own VLA/foundation-model training+deploy platform; NVIDIA Isaac as infra, teleop for data, hiring ML teamReframe their foundation-model build as a capital sink they can't out-spend Physical Intelligence/NVIDIA on. Rent the 1-10Hz task-reasoning brain now to ship autonomy quarters faster; refocus their team on arm, data moat, and GTM.A Series B hardware team is trying to staff and fund a foundation-model research org it can't afford; autonomy ships slowly while burn climbs.RaaS at ~$5/hr is ~$10-12k/robot/yr recurring; a thinking-layer at ~15-25% of that = ~$1.5-3k/robot/yr. At an estimated ~few-hundred-robot deployed base, ~$150-400k/yr today; scales toward ~$1M+ as fleet and multi-shift hours grow. Cushioned by $63M raise.Per-robot-hour (or % of their RaaS hour), e.g. $0.50-1.00/robot-hr, optionally + per-decision API for cloud reasoning. Mirrors their own $5/hr RaaS billing, so the brain cost rides their existing usage meter and margin model.
AgtonomyBuilds own perception/localization/planning stack; dedicated Autonomy Team, proprietary TrunkVision + Physical AI; remote-supervised hybrid-cloud delivery.They build perception/nav themselves but lack a frontier reasoning org. Sell the cloud "thinking layer" as the 1-10Hz planning brain above their edge perception — rent capability they can't afford to staff, freeing them to ship OEM coverage.A ~$64M Series B operator can't staff a frontier reasoning/planning research org while racing OEM integrations across crops, turf and two continents.~$80-200k/yr today at pilot-to-early-commercial fleets (tens of supervised tractors across Bobcat/Kubota pilots); scales to ~$500k-1M/yr as fleets reach hundreds across SE US + Australia and per-operator span grows past 10 robots.Per-robot/month subscription with hybrid edge/cloud tiers — mirrors their per-machine OEM/RaaS economics and scales cleanly as one operator's supervised fleet grows past 10 robots.
Dusty RoboticsBuilds own localization/control + path-planning software in-house; hiring Robotics SW Eng + Data Scientist; buys commodity motion HW.Don't touch their 1mm realtime print loop — they'll never outsource it. Wedge the higher-altitude layer: BIM-to-path planning, multi-trade layout sequencing, on-floor replan. Pitch "rent the planning brain instead of hiring 4 ML engineers you can't afford at Series B."Tiny ~59-person team must build BIM-to-floor planning, multi-trade sequencing, and on-site replan while also shipping hardware — feature coverage is the bottleneck, not the print head.$120-250k/yr now, scaling to ~$500k. Grounded in displacing 1-2 autonomy/ML hires (~$300k loaded each) on a ~100-200 robot RaaS fleet; small vs $68.7M raised and a per-project layout line-item revenue model.Platform fee + per-decision/API usage on planning calls. Fits because their value scales with layout volume (25M+ sq ft/qtr) and their own RaaS is usage-proportional, so usage-based planning maps onto their COGS.
Ati MotorsFull-stack in-house: own nav/perception/sensor-fusion + fleet SW, edge on Jetson; now building "Ati Flow" agentic orchestration AIDon't fight onboard autonomy (their USP/edge identity). Target the NEW multi-site, multi-vendor Ati Flow brain they just committed to: rent the cloud reasoning layer so they ship orchestration in months, not a 2-yr platform build.Scaling cloud fleet-orchestration AI across 40-70+ global sites and multi-vendor fleets while a 30-person edge-focused team has no orchestration/research bandwidth.~$150-400k/yr landed. Grounded in ~hundreds of robots (≈300-600) across 40-70 sites, ~$8.9M revenue, $37M raised; orchestration brain priced as a fraction of their ~$1.5-3k/robot/mo RaaS. Scales toward ~$1M+ as fleet passes 1,000 robots and multi-vendor orchestration expands.Per-robot/month for the orchestration brain (e.g. $50-90/robot/mo), tiered by fleet size, plus a multi-vendor-robot add-on. Why: mirrors their own RaaS billing, scales with fleet/site growth, reads as a small line-item beside their robot RaaS not a competing platform.
Bonsai RoboticsBuilds own VLA + perception transformers; OpenCV founder as CSO; trains on 30M+ samples via AnyscaleDon't fight their VLA — it's their moat. Wedge as the cloud fleet-reasoning + mixed-OEM orchestration layer their edge stack can't do: cross-fleet job planning, fleet-wide learning, non-Amiga machine onboarding. Offload non-moat compute.A $15M Series A can't staff frontier edge VLA AND build cloud fleet-scale reasoning/orchestration across mixed OEM machines; engineering bandwidth is the constraint.Low and hard-won. $15M raised, ~45-100 units near-term, self-funds core AI. Wedge pilot $50-120k/yr; scales to ~$300-400k/yr only past a few hundred mixed-fleet machines.Per-robot/month for the cloud orchestration tier, plus per-decision/API metering on burst fleet-planning calls — mirrors their existing per-Amiga SaaS model and prices only the cloud reasoning they don't build, avoiding competition with their owned edge inference.
CanvasProprietary onboard CV + ML surface-mapping and room-level finish-path planning; ex-Boston Dynamics/SRI/MIT robotics team.Target is now JLG/Oshkosh, not standalone Canvas. Sell the cloud brain as the multi-trade reasoning layer that scales Canvas's drywall stack to new trades (paint, layout, demo) without re-staffing a planning research org per trade.Each new trade/jobsite needs fresh perception+finish-path planning; re-staffing a planning research org per application is slow and costly post-acquisition.Standalone Canvas-scale (est. 20-60 robots): $150-400k/yr. As JLG/Oshkosh multi-trade platform play, $500k-2M+/yr enterprise/platform deal; high uncertainty given fresh strategic ownership.Platform fee + per-robot-hour usage. Fits a RaaS/lease operator (already bills "like a car lease") — usage scales with billable robot hours, platform fee anchors the multi-trade roadmap commitment.
TeleoBuilds own perception engine + path-planning + control; hiring to move hand-tuned MPC to learning-based policies.Land the autonomy gap, not the build: they run hand-tuned MPC + teleop today and are just now staffing learning-based control. Rent a ready planning/control brain to ship per-task autonomy faster than a 1-2 person ML team can.Series A team can't staff a frontier autonomy org; stuck on hand-tuned MPC + teleop, slow to expand autonomous task coverage across machine types.~$80-180k/yr now at a deployed fleet of ~30-60 machines (order book ~70-100); scales to ~$400-700k/yr as fleet and autonomous-task coverage grow. Grounded in $29.8M Series A scale and labor-replacement economics (an operator runs many machines).Per-robot/month platform fee + per-decision usage on autonomous tasks; mirrors their RaaS subscription and prices the autonomy value, not idle teleop.
Persona AIBuilding own autonomy: dedicated ML/autonomy team training manipulation policies (VLA, diffusion, behavior cloning) in-house.Exploit their own framing: Pratt downplays software as the moat (actuation is) and already borrows surrogate robots + SOTA models. Pitch the cloud brain as the "borrow" that frees the ML team for welding-specific skills, not infra.A ~$42M seed team is funding a full ML/autonomy org (VLA training, sim, data pipelines, fleet inference) that burns runway not spent on hardware and the HD Hyundai deadline.~$60-150k/yr through 2027 at pilot scale (single-digit to low-tens of units, pre-commercial); scales to ~$300-600k/yr as HD Hyundai shipyard fleets reach dozens-to-low-hundreds. Capped by their ~$42M seed runway and single anchor customer.Platform fee + per-robot/month usage. Low platform fee fits a budget-constrained seed; per-robot/month scales cleanly with the HD Hyundai fleet rollout and is predictable for their RaaS/contract economics versus per-decision unpredictability at industrial duty cycles.
AscentoETH-Zurich team builds proprietary perception/autonomy on NVIDIA Jetson edge; remote-monitoring human-in-loopDon't fight their edge stack — sell the cloud reasoning tier above it: fleet-wide semantic threat understanding, anomaly generalization, multi-robot coordination a 10-person team can't keep advancing while scaling hardware + 2-continent GTM.Tiny team must scale fleet, hardware reliability, and US+EU GTM while also advancing the autonomy/threat-reasoning brain — they cannot do all four.~$60-120k/yr now at an est. 30-60 robot fleet (post-2024 doubling); scales toward $300-500k/yr as fleet crosses several hundred units. Capped by ~$4.8M raised and a 10-person team — must read as cheaper than 2-3 ML hires (~$600k+/yr loaded).Per-robot/month platform fee plus per-decision usage on alert/edge-case verification. It mirrors their hired-by-the-hour RaaS unit economics, scales cost 1:1 with the billable patrol fleet, and isolates a clean cloud-brain COGS line they can pass through to security customers.
ZippediProprietary patented nav (SLAM/auto-map + dynamic replan) + own deep-learning CV; ML on GCP Vertex AI; 50+ eng in Chile.Don't attack their CV moat. Wedge the slow aisle-nav reasoning + planogram-reasoning layer: offload that to us so their scarce Chilean ML team focuses on CV/SKU models and chainwide GTM, cutting time-to-coverage and GCP burn.Scaling nav-reasoning + planogram logic across thousands of new aisles/stores requires headcount and GCP spend they can't grow linearly in a Series-A budget.~$120-250k/yr at current ~100-380 robots; scales toward ~$600k-1M/yr as they push chainwide across LatAm + US (1000+ robots), tied to ~$22.7M raise and per-store RaaS economics.Per-robot/month hybrid platform+usage
FlytrexVertically integrated: own drones + proprietary AI flight logic, cloud fleet mgmt, route planning, 80M-backyard map DBDon't fight their flight-control IP (their moat). Wedge on the cloud planning/dispatch layer: rent fleet-level routing/multi-drone deconfliction so their TA team stays focused on safety-critical flight + hardware as they scale to 60+ sites.Scaling from a few sites to 60+ metros multiplies fleet routing/dispatch/airspace-deconfliction complexity faster than a single Tel Aviv backend team can build it.$150-400k/yr near-term (DFW ~300-600 drones across 60 sites by 2027), scaling to $1-2M/yr at nationwide multi-metro fleets; bounded by thin per-delivery unit economics and $60M+Uber fundingPer-robot/month + per-decision usage
PerceptoBuilds own perception/AI in-house: CV algorithm engineers, proprietary "AI change detection" on cloud AIM platformThey built perception (the eyes), not the decision brain. Don't fight their CV team — own the reasoning/re-tasking layer they haven't staffed: pitch TCO of a planning-AI team vs renting it, and faster closed-loop autonomy without diverting CV roadmap.Pre-programmed missions can't react: a detected anomaly can't auto-trigger a closer-look flight or re-prioritize the fleet without a human in the loop.~$120-250k/yr today across an estimated tens-to-low-hundreds of deployed boxes (~$200-400/box/mo on ~50-100 boxes); scales to ~$600k-1M/yr as enterprise BVLOS fleets (up to 30 drones/operator) expand across Fortune 500 sites. Grounded in $134M raised, ~182 staff, RaaS economics.Per-robot/month platform fee + per-decision usage (anomaly-triggered re-tasks / investigative missions). Mirrors their existing per-box RaaS subscription billing and ties our cost to value-events (anomalies acted on), not raw flight hours.
KeyboticBuilds own edge SLAM/nav + AI anomaly detection on-robot; ex-PAL/DARPA SubT core team; now owned by Helsing.Hard target now: Helsing supplies the brain internally and won't outsource the thinking layer in defense. Only realistic wedge: license our cloud anomaly/fleet-reasoning to their civilian industrial-inspection line as a non-defense, faster-to-coverage stopgap.15-person team can't scale per-site anomaly models + fleet learning across many plants while also serving Helsing's defense roadmap; coverage breadth lags.Low pre-acquisition: ~$30-80k/yr at sub-20 robot industrial fleet. Post-Helsing, defense path is closed; realistic civilian-line deal $50-120k/yr, only if Helsing greenlights an external brain (unlikely).Per-robot/month + per-decision API overage. Fits a small RaaS fleet: aligns our cost to their billed robot-hours, low commit to clear procurement at a 15-person, capital-light operator.
Path RoboticsBuilt Obsidian foundation welding model + Weld World neural-sim; full in-house ML/perception/RL org; edge-deployed brainDo not attack Obsidian (their moat/fundraise story). Sell adjacent: cloud train/sim compute + fleet-scale offboard reasoning they won't staff a platform team for, and fast generalization to non-weld manufacturing tasks beyond their welding model.Edge-only welding model can't generalize to new manufacturing tasks fast; world-model sim + MLOps infra is a costly platform team distraction from welding/GTM.Low base, high ceiling: ~$150-400k/yr for cloud sim/training + fleet reasoning infra at current scale (~$271M raised, ~198 staff, early fleet); could scale to ~$1M+ only if they expand past weld into multi-task manufacturing AI. Most are unlikely to buy — they are the in-house benchmark.Platform fee + usage (GPU-hours for world-model training/sim + per-decision offboard reasoning calls). Fits because their value lives in proprietary models/data; they'll only rent undifferentiated compute and fleet-orchestration infra, billed to usage they already meter via Foundry.
YarboBuilds proprietary PPVS: RTK+stereo-vision+AI segmentation perception/nav, in-house Shenzhen R&D, monthly OTA, no autonomy vendor.Don't replace their edge nav; sell the cloud reasoning brain they can't staff. Frame in-house semantic planning as opportunity cost vs hardware/GTM; their documented obstacle-avoidance failures are the wedge. Plug into their Open API.Their nav works but reasoning fails: inconsistent obstacle avoidance (hit a truck), weed false-stops, RTK boundary excursions, immature semantic classification — safety/trust risk at scale.~$150-400k/yr near-term: ~10-20k active units at $0.5-1.50/unit/mo for cloud reasoning + planning APIs; scales toward $1M+/yr as fleet crosses 50k+ units across NA/EU/Japan/Australia expansion.Per-robot/month (tiered, with per-decision/API burst): fits a consumer hardware unit-sale business — predictable per-unit cost they can fold into an "intelligence" subscription to owners, with usage upside as the fleet scales.
Hybrid — buys part of the stack, builds the rest · 7
Kärcher (KIRA)Migrated off licensed BrainOS toward self-integrated stack: own software/cloud + SICK safety sensors; small internal robotics teamDisplace their post-BrainOS DIY stitching of SICK sensors + ROS scripts. Frame: you bolted together a brain you can't scale across B50/B200/CV50; rent one cloud reasoning layer, redeploy ROS engineers to hardware/GTM, cut per-site commissioning time.Self-built nav stack scales poorly across a growing multi-model KIRA fleet; slow per-site mapping/commissioning and thin reasoning vs robot-native rivals.~$150-400k/yr entry at a few hundred fielded KIRA units; scales to $1-3M/yr as the B50/B100/B200/CV50 fleet reaches low-thousands across 85 countries, given €3.44B parent and strategic autonomy priority.Per-robot/month platform fee + cloud-reasoning usage
Rice RoboticsIntegrates NVIDIA/Jetson nav stack + own fleet cloud; dialog/companion "brain" announced, not built (token-financed)Frame the token-funded "AGI Foundry" as a multi-year, unstaffable money pit. Rent them a production dialog+planning brain now to hit the Q3-2026 M1 launch; let the token fund hardware/data, not a research team they lack.No ML research org to actually build the companion dialog/planning brain they've publicly promised, with a Q3-2026 consumer launch deadline looming.~$60-120k/yr land (dialog pilot on M1 + existing 500-robot fleet); expands to ~$300-500k/yr as M1 consumer units and conversation volume scale. Capital-constrained ($7M seed, ~$11.6M total, +HK$15M/~$1.9M JV), so deals must stay a small fraction of their ~$500-1,500/robot/mo RaaS economics.Hybrid platform fee + per-decision/API usage: low platform floor suits a cash-tight seed firm, and companion-dialog volume (per-interaction) is the natural meter for chatty M1 units; Jetson keeps latency-critical nav on-edge while cloud bills the 1-10 Hz reasoning.
Andromeda Robotics (Abi)Buys LLMs (GPT-4 + multi-LLM) glued by in-house "Andromeda OS"; builds nav/motion autonomy in-houseTarget the social-reasoning brain they hand-orchestrate, not their nav build. Pitch: stop staffing a dialogue/memory/orchestration team — rent a managed empathetic-reasoning layer so engineering goes to hardware + GTM and US scale-up.Series A engineers burn cycles maintaining bespoke multi-LLM orchestration, memory, sentiment routing and offline fallback instead of hardware/GTM at 100 units/mo scale.~$60-120k/yr today (~tens of active units across AU + US pilots); scales to ~$400-700k/yr as US waitlist + 100 units/mo ramp pushes fleet into the hundreds. Anchored to ~$10k BOM, RaaS economics, and $23M raise that must fund hardware not a research team.Per-robot-hour (or per-engagement-session). Their value and RaaS billing are both engagement-hour-denominated (3,500 hrs logged), so usage-priced reasoning maps directly to their revenue and the offline/online split, keeping COGS proportional to resident interaction.
Enchanted Tools (Mirokaï)In-house RL/learning-from-demo for control+nav (own VSLAM); rents LLMs/VLMs via DIY "multi-LLM agnostic" orchestration on Jetson.They already rent the LLM layer ad hoc — displace the brittle DIY multi-LLM glue with our managed cloud reasoning layer. Pitch: stop maintaining orchestration; redeploy scarce ML staff onto manipulation/expressive control only they can build.DIY multi-LLM orchestration for dialog + expressive social reasoning is fragile, latency-sensitive, and burns scarce seed-stage ML headcount needed on hardware/control.~$60-120k/yr now at ~25-40 fielded/pilot units; scales to ~$400-700k/yr as the fleet crosses a few hundred robots. Anchored to ~$40k hardware ASP — ~$200-400/robot/mo is a credible 10-15% of unit value and far cheaper than 2-3 Paris ML FTEs (~$400k+/yr loaded).Per-robot/month platform fee + metered usage (per reasoning call / token-equiv). Why: per-robot/month aligns to their per-unit RaaS economics for predictable opex; usage metering captures heavy social-interaction sessions without penalizing idle units, fitting a cash-constrained seed-stage fielding model.
Onward RoboticsLicenses RGo perception engine; builds Pyxis orchestration/fleet-planning brain in-houseThey already rent cognition (RGo perception) — use that precedent. Offload the Pyxis Conductor fleet/multi-agent planning brain to us so their ~93-person team funds GTM and hardware, not a planning research group they can't afford to staff at Series A.At Series A, an in-house multi-agent fleet-coordination/planning team is a cash-and-focus drain that slows new-site time-to-value and per-site margin.~$20-60k/yr per ~30-robot site today (3-5 live sites = ~$80-250k/yr); scales toward ~$400-700k/yr as deployments grow. Anchored to AMR RaaS of ~$1.5-4k/robot/mo where a thinking-layer slice is 5-15%.% of their RaaS contract (~5-15% of per-robot/month). It scales with billed fleet, stays a fixed pass-through margin line, and matches how they already embed RGo-style cognition cost into RaaS pricing.
Reflex RoboticsTeleop-first in live RaaS pilots today; standing up in-house AI team (World-Models + RL) to build own autonomy brain.Intercept the build before the two Principal-AI hires harden into a sunk-cost research org: show that a serious world-models+RL+sim+infra effort starts around $0.5-1.5M/yr for the first 2-4 engineers and climbs toward several million per year as it hardens into a full research org, versus renting our brain now, redirecting their $7M to hardware/GTM and GXO ramp.Seed budget can't fund a world-models+RL+sim research org while also building hardware and ramping the GXO RaaS pilot to autonomy.$60-120k/yr at current ~10-30 robot pilot scale; scales to $300-600k/yr as GXO/Fortune-100 ramp pushes the fleet toward 100+ robots over 2026-27.per-robot-hour blended with platform fee
TaurobIn-house nav/teach-and-repeat (EasyMission, Admiralty) + buys higher-order AI from Energy Robotics/KalypsoDisplace Energy Robotics/Kalypso as the reasoning layer: show deeper hybrid edge/cloud autonomy with ATEX-aware planning, lower TCO than maintaining their own EasyMission stack + paying ER, and faster site-coverage so their tiny team focuses on certified hardware + RaaS GTM.A ~50-person hardware team splits scarce engineers between an aging teach-and-repeat stack and a third-party brain (Energy Robotics) it doesn't control or differentiate on.~$120-250k/yr now across a deployed fleet of ~15-40 ATEX robot-sites (offshore O&M RaaS runs $150-500k/robot-yr, so a thinking-layer slice is defensible); scales to ~$500k-1M as Dietsmann/Rockwell channels expand the fleet.Per-robot/month platform fee + per-decision/mission usage. Fits their own RaaS subscription model and tiered packages, lets them mark up the brain inside high-value offshore RaaS contracts while keeping CtoS predictable.
Buying from a vendor today · 3
Tennant CompanyExclusively licenses Brain Corp BrainOS/SelfPath AI as its autonomy brain; $32M equity stake + board seatDon't fight the fresh 3-yr exclusive head-on. Wedge into what BrainOS Clean can't do: outdoor/multi-surface, fleet-level orchestration, new robot form factors. Position as Tennant's single-vendor-lock-in hedge for its $250M bet.Single-vendor dependency: a public company is betting a $250M business on one supplier it part-owns, with autonomy confined to indoor floor-cleaning routes.AMR line ~$85M (2025), targeting $100M by 2027 and $250M by 2028; ~10k robots sold, growing fast. A wedge on new/expansion robots realistically lands $300-600k/yr in 2-3 yrs (a few hundred robots), scaling toward $1.5-3M+/yr if it becomes the brain for the non-cleaning or outdoor portfolio.Per-robot/month subscription mirroring their own Clean360 bundle. Tennant already resells autonomy as monthly recurring revenue with a 90% uptime guarantee, so a per-robot/month brain layer slots into their existing P&L and customer billing motion.
NilfiskLicenses autonomy from multiple vendors: Carnegie Robotics, Brain Corp/BrainOS, LionsBot. Explicit multi-partner strategy, no in-house brain.Displace the fragmented stack: pitch one cloud reasoning layer across all SKUs (replacing per-vendor lock-in to Brain/Carnegie/LionsBot), with a clean switching path on next platform refresh and a stronger hybrid edge/cloud + fleet-analytics story.Three separate autonomy vendors per product line: fragmented capability, no unified fleet brain, vendor lock-in, margin leakage on royalties, slow cross-SKU feature parity.~$300k-800k/yr initially (autonomous fleet est. low-thousands of units at ~$150-250/robot/mo); scales to $1.5-3M/yr as Freudenberg-era autonomous mix grows across €1B+ revenue base and 100+ countries.Per-robot/month + usage tier
ICE CoboticsLicenses Brain Corp's BrainOS for Cobi 18 autonomy; owns only i-SYNERGY fleet/data layer + RaaS GTM.Displace Brain Corp: their BrainOS is single-OEM lock-in and an opaque per-robot royalty. Offer lower per-robot cognition cost, multi-skill coverage, and a hybrid edge/cloud brain ICE can co-brand under i-SYNERGY.Brain Corp royalty is a fixed per-robot tax on every RaaS unit, capping margin; ICE owns the customer/data but rents the brain it can't control or differentiate.~$3-6k/robot/yr for cognition (undercutting BrainOS royalty). At ~500-1,000 active Cobi units today: ~$1.5-5M/yr; scales toward $10M+ as Kum & Go (400+ stores) and Phillips 66 (7,000+ locations) roll out. Tempered by ~$7.7M revenue — early deal likely $150-400k pilot.Per-robot/month or % of RaaS contract: directly swaps the Brain Corp per-unit royalty and aligns with their <€20/day all-inclusive subscription billing, keeping a clean per-unit margin story.

Where to hunt — segments ranked.

Tier A — Hunt here first (strong-rated, cleanest cloud-fit):

7. Commercial cleaning (strong) — the single clearest buy-not-build segment: BrainOS already licenses a thinking layer to Tennant, Nilfisk, SoftBank, ICE and RPS. Coverage planning, re-routing and fleet coordination at 1-10 Hz on slow indoor machines with no safety-critical real-time dependency. A long tail of hardware-first scrubber/sweeper OEMs with no autonomy org. 20. Social / service / elder-care (strong) — the differentiating "thinking" here is conversation, social/affective reasoning and task planning, which runs natively in the cloud; a fat tail of Seed-A/B companion and service-robot makers with no research org, many already bolting on generic LLMs.

Tier B — Core moderate veins (good cloud-fit, sweet-spot funding):

3 / 2. Stationary latency-tolerant manipulation & AI pick-pack (moderate) — pick cycles are seconds not milliseconds; truck-unloading, parcel and food-assembly operators (Anyware, Contoro, Chef, Pickle) race to ship without a VLA org. 6. Warehouse AMRs (moderate) — fleet scheduling, multi-robot path planning and pick/place reasoning map cleanly to a cloud/hybrid brain; dense Seed-C OEM tail (Onward, Ati Motors, Gideon, Capra). 14. Construction (moderate) — layout, drywall, rebar, painting and supervised-autonomy retrofit task robots all tolerate 1-10 Hz hybrid planning (Dusty, Canvas, Monumental, Teleo, Rugged). 4. Cobots (moderate) — a skill/retrofit layer is already sold by outsiders, proving buyers will rent; affordable/no-code arm OEMs (Standard Bots, Niryo, Ally) compete on ease-of-deployment, not autonomy IP. 13. Agricultural / field (moderate) — tele-assisted/supervised autonomy in connected sub-niches (greenhouses, orchards, dairy) relaxes the rural-connectivity blocker (Agtonomy, Bonsai, Four Growers).

Tier C — Selective moderate (good fit but diluted or geography/overlap-discounted):

16. Humanoids (moderate) — canonically hybrid TL; a long tail of mid-funded hardware-first OEMs (Persona, Reflex, Foundation, Dexmate), but the loudest names are builders/competitors. 18. Drones / UAVs (moderate) — a real commercial pocket (delivery, drone-in-a-box, ag, inventory) sits inside a segment half-dominated by regulation-locked defense and competitor autonomy-software. 9. Retail shelf-scan (moderate) — good cloud-fit but ~half the field is fixed-camera CV vendors; real pool is a dozen funded robot OEMs (Zippedi, Pensa, Adapta). 10. Security patrol (moderate) — solid technical fit but every deal must clear the surveillance-feed cloud/on-prem objection, with a police/defense regulation-locked tail. 17. Quadruped inspection (moderate) — detachable inspection brain (Energy Robotics/Levatas prove it), but thin once you remove defense, China-cloud and competitor autonomy-software. 1. Fixed industrial arms (moderate) — only the thin application-layer slice (AI welders, no-code, adaptive arms) is in play; mega-OEMs own their stacks.

Tier D — Do not hunt (weak/none-rated, structurally blocked):

5. Surgical/medical (none) and 22. Exoskeletons (none) — regulation-locked; the stack must be validated, owned and onboard. 24. Space/planetary (none) — onboard-only under intermittent, light-delayed comms; export-controlled. 12. Road delivery/robotaxis (weak) — the driving brain is structurally onboard-only (safety). 11. Sidewalk delivery (weak) and 21. Telepresence (weak) — autonomy IS the product (operators build it) or the remote human IS the thinking layer. 8. Hospital/indoor logistics (weak) — sold as closed RaaS with HIPAA/on-prem pressure. 15. Mining/subsea/hazardous (weak) — comms-denied AUVs and mega-OEM-owned haulage; only a thin tethered-ROV/surface-mining tail. 23. Defense/military (weak) — EW-contested comms-denial plus sovereignty/ITAR force an owned onboard stack. 19. Consumer home (weak) — sealed $200-1500 appliances run the brain on-device for cost/privacy; only the commercial/outdoor-service fringe is viable.

Who is not an ICP.

1. Vertically-integrated frontier builders — they build the brain as their moat, never rent it. Humanoids: Tesla (Optimus), Figure AI ($1.9B, Helix), Apptronik ($935M), 1X Technologies, Boston Dynamics. Pick/manipulation: Dexterity ($300M, Mech), Nimble ($221M), Symbotic ($1B), Berkshire Grey. AVs: Waymo, Zoox, Cruise, Aurora, Tesla FSD, Baidu Apollo. AMR leaders: Locus, Geek+, AutoStore, Exotec, Amazon Robotics. Cleaning: Gausium ($304M), Pudu ($300M), Avidbots ($107M). These have the cash and the research org to build, and usually do.

2. Autonomy / perception / foundation-model software vendors — they ARE the product a "fal for robotics" sells, so they are competitors, not customers. Robot foundation models: Physical Intelligence, Skild AI ($1.8B), Covariant (RFM-1), Sereact, Dyna Robotics, NVIDIA Isaac GR00T, Google DeepMind Gemini Robotics, Field AI, Generalist AI. Bin-picking/perception SW: Mujin ($411M), Osaro, Plus One, Fizyr, Apera, Mech-Mind, XYZ Robotics, RightHand. Navigation/orchestration: Brain Corp (BrainOS), Carnegie Robotics, LionsBot, Sevensense, Meili Robots, GreyOrange (GreyMatter), BlueBotics, Levatas, Energy Robotics, Auterion, DroneDeploy, Skydio. AV/off-highway stack licensors: Mobileye, Nuro, Plus, Momenta, Wayve, Polymath, ASI, SafeAI, Overland AI, Forterra, Kodiak.

3. Component / sensor / actuator suppliers — they sell parts, not robots, so there is no thinking layer to rent. Vision/sensors: Photoneo, Zivid, Hikrobot, Roboception, e-con Systems, Sonardyne. Tooling/hands/actuators: OnRobot, Inspire Robots, Shadow Robot, Wonik (Allegro), HIWIN, ROBOTIS, Motiv Space. Infrastructure: Hextronics, Skycharge (drone docks), FORT Robotics (safety), Nokia (5G).

4. Mega-OEMs & conglomerate subsidiaries — too big, own the full controller/software stack in-house. FANUC, ABB, KUKA, Yaskawa, Universal Robots (Teradyne), Stäubli, Mitsubishi, DENSO, Comau; medtech Intuitive Surgical, Medtronic, Stryker, J&J MedTech; ag John Deere, CNH, AGCO, Kubota; construction/mining Caterpillar, Komatsu, Sandvik, Trimble, Hexagon; defense primes Anduril, Lockheed, BAE, Northrop, Saronic ($2.58B); conglomerate units Dematic (KION), Swisslog (KUKA), Magazino (Jungheinrich), Sevensense (ABB).

5. Regulation-locked & comms-denied operators — surgical robots, exoskeletons, pharmacy/chemo compounding (Omnicell, BD Rowa), space servicing (Astroscale, Northrop SpaceLogistics), defense UGV/USV combat systems, and subsea AUVs must own a validated, onboard, often air-gapped stack; a continuously-updated third-party cloud brain is architecturally and legally incompatible.

Caveats — read before acting on this.

Funding data is materially incomplete. A large share of the most "buy-not-build"-shaped companies — bootstrapped/private OEMs (most of commercial cleaning: Tennant, Kärcher, Nilfisk, RPS, Hako, Comac, Fimap), undisclosed-funding makers, and many seed players — have null funding figures, so the "can pay" half of the thesis is unverified for them. Roughly 40-45% of named targets lack a disclosed funding number; ability-to-pay is inferred from stage/segment, not confirmed.

The two best-fit segments are the smallest pools. The strong-rated beachheads (commercial cleaning, social/service) are genuinely fertile but narrow: in cleaning, the biggest names are public/private incumbents — build-the-machine, rent-the-intelligence is proven but the buyer count is finite — and social/care skews to thinly-funded seed companies where ability-to-pay is the binding constraint. Several of the largest segments by company count (drones, AMRs, defense, subsea) are mostly NON-ICP once builders, competitors, suppliers and regulation-locked players are removed.

Many high-scoring targets carry partial-overlap or competitor risk. Path Robotics ($271M), Nimble ($221M), Telexistence, Simbe and NEURA are at or past the build-vs-buy line and may build in-house; Augmentus and Wandelbots are themselves programming-software vendors (partial competitors). Scores in the 60s-70s are explicitly hedged in the source data.

Geography injects a cloud-data-residency tax. China targets (Elite, AUBO, JAKA, FAIRINO, Rokae, Robot Era, Pudu, Multiway, Guozi) score lower because of data-locality pressure and domestic-vendor/foundation-model preference; a Western cloud brain may be a poor fit regardless of the technical match.

"Cloud-fit" is hybrid, not pure-cloud, almost everywhere. Fast control stays onboard in every segment; only the 1-10 Hz reasoning tier is rentable, and even that often wants an on-prem/edge option (hospital HIPAA, security surveillance feeds, China residency). The product must support hybrid deployment, and every deal in security/patrol and hospital must clear an on-prem objection.

The screen is a desk-research snapshot, not validated demand. Fit scores reflect inferred build-vs-buy economics and architecture, not confirmed buying intent, budget authority, or a stated willingness to depend on a third-party cloud for autonomy — all of which require direct discovery before treating any named target as qualified.

Who's already doing this#

The companies already in or adjacent to this space, grouped by which layer they sell.

CompanyLayerStage / fundingTraction
Model + serving + fleet (vertically integrated — proven)
Sereact · Mbodi · Flexion · GRIDVLA + fleet learning / NL→action / intelligence layer$110M B · YC X25 · $50M A (NVIDIA) · Accenture+MSFTSereact 1B+ picks, BMW/PepsiCo; Mbodi ABB + 5,000-robot pilot
Pure VLA-serving wedge — the literal "fal for robotics" play (nascent; product boundaries and traction unverified)
FastCrest (tryreflex.ai) · Qualia · phospho · Hebbian · DexmalVLA deploy / serve / optimize~$0–7.5M; mostly unverifiedOSS-led; no named paying customers verified as of May 2026 — which cuts both ways: the market may be early and open, or no one will pay for this as a standalone product. Product scope, differentiation from generic GPU clouds, and traction are largely unverified from public sources.
General GPU clouds already serving robot AI inference
Modal · Nebiusserverless / managed GPU cloud~$1.1B val / publicModal already runs Physical Intelligence's VLA inference 24/7 in production — a general GPU cloud, not a robotics specialist, is already the incumbent for at least one leading VLA workload; Together.ai and similar providers are equally available to robot teams today, no robotics-specific moat required.
Adjacent — fleet-ops / data
Foxglove · Config · Formant · InOrbitobservability / data / RobOps$40M · $27M · ~$39M · ~$13M"data infra for physical AI"
Incumbent floor (free) + model labs (compete from above)
NVIDIA (GR00T/NIM/Jetson/Isaac) · HF LeRobot · openpichip+model+runtime / OSS policy serverpublic / company-backed"Android of generalist robotics"; sets the price at $0
Physical Intelligence · Skild · FieldAI · Mind · Rhoda · AgiBotrobot foundation models + deployPI ~$11B · Skild ~$14B · FieldAI $405M · Rhoda $450Mown weights + flywheel; gate via API/partnership
Emerging autonomy / perception / inspection vendors — Korea (sample batch; mostly early-stage)
Sonnet.AI · Seoul Robotics · Vueron · Daim Research · Neural D · Guardian AI · Sequor Roboticsautonomy SW / 3D perception / fleet-OS & swarm / inspection AI / VLM safety / robot-training infraearly-stage (KR)independent thinking-layer / autonomy vendors — the competitor set, not customers

Reference — every term and decision point#

Definitions for everything on this page, with the numbers and sources behind them.

TermDefinitionSource
Three inference layersFast control (reflex) 50–1000 Hz, sub-50 ms, no buffer → onboard. Thinking layer (planning/perception) 1–10 Hz, tolerates +200–300 ms via action-chunking → cloud-eligible. Training/fleet-learning → cloud.RTC
A1 maturity / A6.S sunsetStage by program: bootstrapping (under ~10 units) / pilot (10–100) / scaled (over 100). Every leaf is transitional with re-eval triggers (T1–T12) except scaled or fixed-by-law. Full tables in the reference section at the end.reference section at the end
A2 hard bar (by remedy)ITAR → access-controlled US region; localization (PIPL ≥1M → CAC) → in-region/sovereign cloud; biometric (BIPA $1k/$5k, GDPR Art.9) → onboard or on-device anonymize; FDA in-loop → change-control; only must-process-locally → onboard.ITAR · PIPL
A2.5 connectivity improvable?Asked only when A3 connectivity is inadequate. Fixed sites can provision private-5G/CBRS (small warehouse $50–100K; base station $100–200K) to reach reliable → continue down the cloud/hybrid path; mobile/outdoor can't → the model must fit onboard (A5).maturity reference section (T5)
A3 connectivity (4-dim)Reliable = p99 RTT ≤50 ms · jitter ≤20 ms · uplink ≥10 Mbps (illustrative for a 3-cam stream; high-res / LiDAR / many-camera payloads need materially more, ≈2–10 Mbps+ ) · loss under 0.1% → wired / private-5G (URLLC sub-1 ms, 99.999%). Intermittent = p99 fails. None = ≤4G (30–70 ms).URLLC
A3.5 model familyAutoregressive (RT-2, OpenVLA): coarse chunks, INT4-friendly. Flow/diffusion (π0, GR00T, Helix): fine action chunks executed at 50–200 Hz (VLA inference itself still 1–10 Hz), quantize only via QuantVLA/QVLA. Discrete-diffusion: chunkable, AR-like quant (most onboard-friendly).RTC
A4.H HYBRID failoverHYBRID stands only if a certified onboard abort policy exists (watchdog ≤1 control cycle @50–200 Hz → Cat-3 PL-d monitored stop); uncertified → power-&-force-limited regimes only; none → HYBRID collapses to onboard-or-nothing.ISO 13849-1 · ISO/TS 15066
A5 compute fitMemory ≈ params × 2 B (FP16) / × 0.5 B (INT4): 3B ≈ 6/1.5 GB, 7B ≈ 14/3.5 GB. Full-onboard = FP16 ≤ VRAM (Orin 32–64 / Thor 128 GB) AND ≥ required inference rate (Thor ~19 Hz on 3B; H100 ~162) within 40–130 W. Else quantized-onboard or offload.Thor · VLA-Perf
A6 offload tiersMenu of off-robot placements (a parameter, not a fork): on-prem-edge (sub-ms, no WAN) · near-edge / MEC (~10–14 ms) · far-cloud (lowest $/GPU but data egress + sovereignty) · hybrid (cloud thinking layer / onboard fast control). All proceed to the A6.SEC fleet-security gate.ETSI MEC
A6.SEC fleet securityA homogeneous fleet on a shared cloud command path is a blast radius (one compromised endpoint → whole fleet). Requires signed commands + per-robot authz + onboard kill-switch; partial → mitigation only; none → security-blocked onboard.maturity reference section (T10)
Build vs buy (Tree B: BX · B2 · BE)Not "do they have a team" — a team is buyable. The BX core-vs-context test (full criteria ⓐ in Filter 2) decides build vs source. B2 (free OSS) only lowers acquisition cost — running it still requires infra, ops, and people, so the build-vs-buy decision still applies. BE economics (full criteria ⓑ in Filter 2): buy wins sub-scale / under ~50–60% sustained util / for speed / volatile requirements; build wins at graduation scale + ≥ ~50–60% util / over ~6 GPU-hr/day (break-even ~13–18 mo).build-vs-rent

Reference — how a robot's needs change over time#

A placement is never permanent. A robot program moves through stages, and specific events push its AI from the cloud toward the robot (or back). First, the stages:

StageQuantified signal (2026)Bias
M1 Bootstrapping — human is the product, robot is the data rigautonomous completion under 80–90%; mean-time-between-interventions under one 8-hr shift; under 10 units (typ 1–5); 1 site; pre-revenue/paid-pilotteleop / hybrid / cloud + data flywheel; capture = full-RaaS or bundled-lab; do not build serving in-house
M2 Piloting — proven at one site, prove it generalizescompletion ≥90–95%; MTBI ≥ one shift→days; 10–100 units, 2–10 sites; serving util under 50% / under 40 GPU-hr/wk; SLAs existnone — run the placement + capture trees exactly as written
M3 Scaling — proven + high sustained volumeover 100–1,000+ homogeneous units, ≥10 sites, own-manufacture at line rate (RoboFab 10K/yr, BotQ 12K/yr, Amazon ~750K–1M); serving util ≥50–60% / over 6 GPU-hr/day; break-even ~13–18 moonboard / quantized-onboard + build-in-house (graduation)

And the events that move a placement — when any of these fires, you re-decide:

#TriggerEffect
T1GPU $/hr crosses ±25% (B200 ~$2.12/hr; H100 ~$1.03–3.12; AWS H200 +15% Jan-26)far-cloud ↔ on-prem-edge / generic-cloud ↔ build
T2model distilled below the onboard-memory line (fits Thor 128 GB at NVFP4 ≥ rate; Shallow-π 2×, SnapFlow 1-step)cloud/hybrid → quantized-onboard / onboard
T3vendor ships an action-head runtime (JetPack 7.1 + TensorRT Edge-LLM on Thor)→ onboard; de-moats the serving vendor
T4fleet crosses build-vs-rent utilization (≥50–60% / over 6 GPU-hr/day; ~13–18 mo break-even)generic-cloud / RaaS → build-in-house
T5connectivity provisioned (private-5G/CBRS: small warehouse $50–100K; base station $100–200K)onboard → hybrid / MEC / on-prem-edge
T6certified failover/abort policy landsunlocks the HYBRID leaf (else it stays collapsed)
T7model family changes (AR Pi0-FAST vs flow π0 2–5 Hz → ~50 Hz via Real-Time Chunking)re-pick A4 chunkability + A5 quantizability
T8autonomy crosses the human-bridge threshold (completion ≥90–95%)stage M1 → M2
T9fleet homogeneity + count cross the in-house bar (over 100–1,000 units / ≥10 sites)stage M2 → M3
T10fleet-correlated blast-radius incident/audit (one endpoint commands the whole fleet)far-cloud → onboard kill-switch + signed commands
T11vendor/licensing economics shift (per-robot license × fleet > in-house; or free-OSS becomes good-enough)licensed/bundled → build-in-house or DIY-free-OSS
T12retrain cadence slows (weekly → quarterly; stable checkpoint)precondition that enables baking an onboard binary

runs off-robot (cloud/edge) · independent business exists hybrid / partial stays onboard · captured / no independent blocked / dead-end a decision
Colour answers two separate questions — where the AI runs (cloud vs onboard) and whether an independent business exists. They don't always agree: a few viable businesses (the defense ones) run onboard.

The honest read: running robot AI off the device is real and broad, but an independent business that does it in the cloud is narrow — about one proven case today (inspection), because a robot's highest-value AI is either the real-time kind that stays on the robot, or something its maker would rather build than rent. The companies that win own the model and the fleet together. Source of truth — edit this file.
